proposals.title: Neuro-Inspired Latency-First Optimization for Deterministic Multi-Agent Services
proposals.motivation: Emergent multi-agent services for real-time control, such as in vehicular digital twins and industrial IoT, are fundamentally constrained by end-to-end communication latency, not just computational throughput. Current resource scheduling and network optimization often treat communication as a secondary resource, leading to suboptimal system determinism and reliability. Drawing a direct analogy from biological neural networks, which evolved myelination and synaptic pruning to prioritize signal propagation speed, suggests a paradigm shift where the communication substrate is the primary design constraint for agent-based systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By architecting multi-agent systems with a 'latency-first' principle, explicitly optimizing the communication fabric through strategic agent placement, predictive messaging, and lightweight protocols, we can achieve significantly higher end-to-end deterministic performance and reliability compared to traditional compute-centric or balanced resource optimization approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical optimization framework. First, a graph-based agent colocation algorithm, inspired by synaptic clustering, will minimize physical network hops by solving a constrained placement problem using pre-2026 metaheuristics or integer programming. Second, we will design a lightweight, header-optimized messaging protocol with predictive push mechanisms based on learned agent interaction patterns (using pre-2026 sequence models like LSTMs). Finally, these components will be integrated and evaluated within a simulated satellite-terrestrial vehicular network testbed, using deterministic latency and service reliability as key metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural blueprint and a concrete optimization framework for latency-critical multi-agent services, directly advancing the field of deterministic networking. It will yield new metrics and algorithms for communication-centric agent scheduling, contributing to more reliable vehicular digital twins and industrial metaverse applications.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the systematic translation of biological neural network optimization principles (prioritizing signal propagation) into a rigorous engineering design paradigm for multi-agent systems, moving beyond traditional network utility maximization to treat the communication fabric as the primary optimization target from the ground up.

proposals.title: Deterministic Latency-Guaranteed Consensus and Scheduling for Critical Services in Byzantine-Prone Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of critical cyber-physical systems, such as vehicular digital twins and industrial control, demands strict end-to-end latency guarantees for control loops and state synchronization. Existing deterministic networking (e.g., DetNet) and consensus protocols (e.g., PBFT) are ill-suited for adversarial environments where Byzantine nodes can deliberately induce delays, creating a fundamental tension between resilience and timeliness. This gap impedes the deployment of reliable, time-critical services in open or contested networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a latency-bounded consensus mechanism with a deterministic, adversarial-aware network scheduler, it is possible to provide provable, deterministic upper bounds on end-to-end communication latency for critical services, even in the presence of a bounded fraction of Byzantine nodes attempting to disrupt timing.
proposals.method: We will first develop a novel consensus protocol, Time-Bounded Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (TB-PBFT), which incorporates a deterministic, time-driven message progression phase to replace indefinite view changes. Second, we will design an intelligent, adversarial-resilient scheduler using reinforcement learning (informed by Value of Information) to orchestrate transmission and computational resources across a converged satellite-terrestrial network topology, explicitly modeling and mitigating delay-inducing attacks. The integrated architecture will be validated via simulation and a hardware testbed emulating vehicular digital twin scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new consensus protocol family that provides deterministic latency guarantees under Byzantine faults, 2) a joint optimization framework for control, communication, and scheduling resilient to timing attacks, and 3) a reference architecture for deterministic, low-latency services in adversarial vehicular and industrial IoT networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit co-design of Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus with deterministic networking, prioritizing bounded-latency message delivery over perfect agreement when necessary, a significant departure from conventional throughput-optimized or safety-first distributed systems.

proposals.title: Communication-Latency-Driven Resource Orchestration for Deterministic Agent Services in Converged Edge Networks
proposals.motivation: Emergent agent services, such as vehicular digital twins and industrial control loops, impose stringent end-to-end latency bounds where communication delay is the dominant and most variable factor. Existing resource optimization frameworks often balance communication and computation, potentially compromising the deterministic latency guarantees required for real-time control and synchronization. A paradigm shift prioritizing communication latency minimization is needed to meet the reliability demands of time-critical cyber-physical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a Value-of-Information (VoI)-driven, communication-centric optimization problem that explicitly subordinates computational resource allocation to the goal of minimizing worst-case communication latency, we can achieve more reliable and deterministic end-to-end service delivery for mobile agents in converged satellite-terrestrial networks.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer hierarchical scheduler. First, a deterministic time-sensitive networking (TSN)-inspired scheduler at the converged network core will allocate guaranteed time slots for agent service flows, using a constrained optimization model to minimize end-to-end transmission delay. Second, a cooperative edge resource manager will solve a VoI-driven sub-problem, dynamically mapping computational tasks to edge servers only after communication paths are secured, employing a primal-dual learning algorithm to adapt to network state variations. The integration will be validated via a Digital Twin network simulation testbed.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel communication-first optimization framework and associated algorithms for deterministic agent services. It will provide rigorous analytical bounds on end-to-end latency and experimental validation of improved reliability in dynamic vehicular and satellite-terrestrial scenarios, advancing the state-of-the-art in converged network control.
proposals.novelty: Unlike joint communication-computation optimization, this work explicitly prioritizes communication latency as the primary constraint, treating computational resource allocation as a secondary, dependent sub-problem. This reverses the typical co-optimization paradigm to directly address the bottleneck in time-critical agent services.

proposals.title: Accuracy-Constrained Pareto Optimization for Multi-Agent Edge Intelligence: A Co-Design of Model Compression and Network Resource Scheduling
proposals.motivation: The deployment of AI models on resource-constrained multi-agent systems, such as vehicular networks and satellite-terrestrial systems, faces a fundamental tension between model accuracy and system efficiency. Existing approaches often optimize model parameters (e.g., pruning) or network resources (e.g., bandwidth) in isolation, leading to sub-optimal global performance. A unified framework is needed to guarantee a minimum application-level accuracy while dynamically trading off secondary metrics like latency, energy, and cost across the entire model-system stack.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating a specified minimum model accuracy as a hard constraint and formulating a joint optimization problem over model-level compression parameters and system-level communication/computation resources, a Pareto-optimal frontier can be constructed that enables extreme adaptability in non-accuracy service qualities without sacrificing core task performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer optimization framework. First, a model profiling layer will characterize the accuracy-resource trade-offs of techniques like quantization and pruning for target tasks. Second, a system optimizer, formulated as a constrained multi-objective problem, will dynamically allocate compute (e.g., via batching) and communication resources (e.g., routing, transmission power) across agents, informed by a Value-of-Information (VoI) metric. We will employ meta-heuristic algorithms (e.g., NSGA-II) or Lyapunov-based online optimization to approximate the Pareto frontier in real-time for dynamic networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel accuracy-constrained co-design optimization formalism for multi-agent intelligence, 2) practical algorithms for constructing and navigating the Pareto frontier of system operating points, and 3) validated performance gains in case studies on vehicular digital twin and satellite-terrestrial sensing networks, demonstrating superior joint efficiency compared to isolated optimizations.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the treatment of minimum accuracy as a hard constraint for a collective of agents, enabling a globally efficient, context-aware exploration of the joint model-system design space, which isolated layer-specific optimizers cannot achieve.

proposals.title: Niche-Driven Dynamic Task Partitioning for Efficient Collaborative Inference in Heterogeneous Edge Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of heterogeneous edge devices (CPUs, GPUs, NPUs) and complex AI models demands collaborative inference, but existing layer-wise model splitting is suboptimal. It fails to exploit the diverse and dynamic computational 'profiles' of devices, leading to inefficient resource utilization and high communication overhead. This problem is critical for latency-sensitive applications like vehicular digital twins and real-time environment sensing in converged networks.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a collaborative device ensemble as a computational ecosystem and applying principles of ecological niche differentiation, we can develop dynamic task partitioning algorithms that assign specialized computational subtasks (niches) to optimally suited devices, thereby maximizing overall system efficiency and reliability.
proposals.method: We will formulate a joint optimization problem for inference latency, energy, and reliability. Inspired by niche theory, we will design a learning-based scheduler that profiles devices for niche-specific fitness (e.g., NPU for quantized ops, GPU for parallel kernels). The core algorithm will be a meta-heuristic (e.g., evolutionary algorithm) or a deep reinforcement learning agent that dynamically partitions the inference graph into niche subtasks and maps them to devices, explicitly trading off computational gain against communication cost, akin to resource scheduling in satellite-terrestrial networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel ecological analogy-based framework for fine-grained, dynamic task partitioning in collaborative inference. It will provide new optimization algorithms and scheduling policies that enhance the efficiency and reliability of edge AI systems. The results will advance the design of intelligent, deterministic networks for future applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the ecological niche theory analogy to define and match computational niches to heterogeneous device profiles, moving beyond static layer splitting. This approach integrates device-specific architectural strengths into a dynamic, system-wide optimization problem for collaborative inference, a perspective not deeply explored in pre-2026 edge AI scheduling.

proposals.title: Resilient Co-Adaptation of Model and Resources under Non-Stationary and Adversarial Conditions
proposals.motivation: The deployment of intelligent agents in real-world networks, such as vehicular digital twins and satellite-terrestrial systems, faces critical challenges from non-stationary environments and potential adversarial perturbations. Existing approaches often treat model optimization and resource scheduling as separate problems, leading to brittle performance when conditions deviate from the training setup. A unified framework for joint, dynamic adaptation is urgently needed to ensure reliability and efficiency in safety-critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a feedback-driven control loop, which jointly and continuously adapts an agent's model parameters and its allocated communication/computation resources based on adversarial simulation and performance monitoring, will achieve significantly higher resilience and operational efficiency in volatile and contested network environments.
proposals.method: We will design a closed-loop system integrating a performance monitor, an adversarial simulator, and a co-adaptation optimizer. The monitor tracks environment shifts and agent performance using metrics like inference accuracy and latency. The simulator, employing adversarial machine learning techniques (e.g., PGD attacks) and non-stationary scenario generation, identifies failure modes. The optimizer, formulated as a constrained Markov Decision Process, will use meta-reinforcement learning or online convex optimization to jointly tune the agent's model (e.g., via pruning or fine-tuning) and its resource schedule (e.g., bandwidth, compute slices).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for the resilient co-adaptation of AI models and network resources, 2) new algorithms for online joint optimization under adversarial and non-stationary constraints, and 3) empirical validation in simulated vehicular digital twin and satellite-terrestrial network testbeds, demonstrating improved robustness and resource efficiency.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty lies in the tight, feedback-driven coupling of model adaptation and resource scheduling within a single control loop, explicitly optimized against adversarially simulated failure modes, moving beyond static optimization or decoupled adaptation strategies prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Federated Mixture of Experts with Strategic Experience Replay for Robust Multi-Agent Learning in Non-IID Vehicular Networks
proposals.motivation: Training intelligent agents in distributed, resource-constrained networks like vehicular digital twins faces the critical challenge of non-IID and heterogeneous local data, which degrades model performance and convergence in standard federated learning. Existing federated reinforcement learning methods struggle to balance the need for specialized local policies with the retention of a robust, globally useful skill set, especially in dynamic environments where catastrophic forgetting is a risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a federated mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture with a privacy-preserving, generative experience replay mechanism will enable collaborative learning of specialized experts while preserving universal competencies, leading to superior and more robust multi-agent policies in non-IID network environments.
proposals.method: We propose a federated learning framework where each agent trains a local MoE model. A federated clustering algorithm (e.g., based on k-means over model gradients or data distributions) groups agents to foster specialized experts. A federated gating network, trained via FedAvg, learns to combine these experts. To mitigate forgetting, a federated variational autoencoder (VAE) is trained on a subset of anonymized local experiences to generate synthetic core data, which is strategically replayed into local training cycles using a prioritized experience replay buffer.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel federated multi-agent learning architecture that explicitly addresses the specialization-forgetting trade-off in non-IID settings. It will provide new algorithms for federated agent clustering and privacy-aware generative replay, advancing the state-of-the-art in reliable and efficient learning for networked intelligent systems like vehicular digital twins.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of federated MoE for specialization and a federated generative model for strategic experience replay, creating a synergistic system for non-IID agent training that is not addressed by prior work in federated learning or multi-agent reinforcement learning alone.

proposals.title: DP-FedMARL: A Differential Privacy Framework for Reliable and Secure Federated Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning in Vehicular Networks
proposals.motivation: Federated learning enables collaborative agent training in distributed systems like vehicular networks, but sharing model updates risks exposing sensitive local driving patterns and environmental data. Pre-2026, the convergence of learning and communication in these networks has prioritized performance and reliability, leaving a critical gap in provable privacy guarantees. This work addresses the urgent need for a training protocol that ensures both reliable collective learning and robust individual data privacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a rigorous differential privacy (DP) mechanism, analogous to statistical disclosure control in census data, into the federated averaging cycle for multi-agent systems will enable the development of a globally robust model while formally bounding the privacy leakage from each participating agent's local updates.
proposals.method: We propose DP-FedMARL, extending the DP-FedAvg algorithm to a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) context. Each agent (e.g., a vehicle) will locally train its policy network, apply gradient clipping for sensitivity bounding, and add calibrated Gaussian noise before transmitting updates to a central aggregator. We will formulate this as a joint optimization problem, balancing the DP privacy budget (epsilon-delta) against model convergence rate and final performance, evaluated within a simulated satellite-terrestrial vehicular network environment for tasks like cooperative sensing and control.
proposals.contribution: This research will yield a novel, privacy-preserving federated MARL protocol with provable DP guarantees for vehicular networks. It will provide a formal framework to quantify and manage the trade-off between privacy, communication efficiency, and learning reliability, directly enhancing the security of collaborative intelligence in next-generation intelligent transportation systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled adaptation of the census-inspired DP framework to the dynamic, sequential decision-making context of federated MARL, moving beyond static data classification to protect agents' unique experiences and policies during cooperative online learning.

proposals.title: Decentralized Orchestration for Resilient Federated Multi-Agent Learning in Vehicular and Satellite Networks
proposals.motivation: Classical federated learning (FL) relies on a central server for orchestration, creating a single point of failure and control, which is unsuitable for fully decentralized, dynamic, and trustless environments like ad-hoc vehicular networks or multi-domain satellite-terrestrial systems. The convergence of autonomous agents and privacy-preserving learning demands new architectures that maintain coordination without centralized infrastructure. This problem is critical for enabling robust, large-scale collaborative intelligence in next-generation networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized orchestration mechanism, using distributed consensus to form transient committees for scheduling and aggregation, can achieve model convergence and system resilience comparable to centralized FL while eliminating its coordination bottleneck and single point of failure.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent system where agents run a lightweight Byzantine-tolerant consensus protocol (e.g., a modified Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance) to self-organize into committees. These committees, formed via verifiable random functions, will propose and validate training rounds, execute a secure aggregation protocol (like SecAgg) for model updates, and manage network membership. The core algorithm extends Federated Averaging (FedAvg) by decentralizing its control plane, integrating it with resource scheduling mechanisms from vehicular digital twins and satellite-terrestrial networks to handle communication constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, fully decentralized FL orchestration framework, theoretically analyzed for convergence and resilience. It will provide a practical system design and evaluation for use cases in vehicular digital twin networks and integrated satellite-terrestrial sensing, advancing the state-of-the-art in privacy-preserving, collaborative learning for autonomous systems.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of federated learning's model aggregation with a decentralized, consensus-based control plane for orchestration is a distinct departure from server-centric FL architectures. It uniquely applies principles from blockchain and distributed systems to the FL coordination problem within heterogeneous wireless networks.

proposals.title: Federated Learning for Decentralized and Reliable Multi-Agent Optimization in Converged Vehicular-Satellite Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of intelligent vehicular and satellite-terrestrial networks demands collaborative agent training for tasks like resource scheduling and environment sensing. However, centralizing data from distributed agents (vehicles, satellites, RSUs) raises significant privacy, communication overhead, and single-point-of-failure concerns. A decentralized, privacy-preserving learning framework is crucial for scalable and reliable network optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored federated learning (FL) framework, incorporating reliability-aware client selection and communication-efficient aggregation, can enable decentralized agents to collaboratively learn superior control and scheduling policies without raw data exchange, matching or exceeding the performance of centralized training.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical FL architecture aligned with the network topology of converged satellite-terrestrial systems. The method integrates: 1) a Value-of-Information (VoI)-based client selection mechanism to prioritize agents with high-quality, relevant data for each global aggregation round, optimizing learning efficiency; 2) the application of compression techniques (e.g., gradient sparsification) and asynchronous aggregation protocols to handle heterogeneous communication latencies, particularly from satellite links; 3) training a shared neural network model for a joint resource scheduling and control problem, with local updates performed on distributed agents using their private sensory data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel FL framework specifically designed for the high-mobility, heterogeneous, and resource-constrained environment of converged vehicular-satellite networks. It will provide a concrete solution for privacy-preserving, collaborative agent training, advancing the field of intelligent network management. The proposed VoI-driven client selection and latency-tolerant aggregation are expected to significantly improve learning convergence speed and final model reliability compared to standard FL approaches.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of FL with network-specific metrics (VoI for client selection) and constraints (satellite-terrestrial transmission delays) for multi-agent optimization, moving beyond generic FL applications to solve concrete networking problems like those in Dr. Shen's recent work on deterministic scheduling and cooperative resource management.

proposals.title: GNN-Embedded Proactive Load Balancing for End-to-End Deterministic Services in Converged Networks
proposals.motivation: The convergence of heterogeneous network domains (e.g., 5G/6G, satellite, edge cloud) and the rise of stringent, end-to-end deterministic services (e.g., industrial IoT, vehicular digital twins) create a complex, dynamic resource orchestration problem. Traditional load balancers, operating on local server metrics, lack the holistic topological awareness to prevent cascading bottlenecks across this multi-domain graph, jeopardizing service-level agreements and resource efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that continuously encoding the dynamic, multi-domain converged infrastructure as a graph and using Graph Neural Network (GNN)-derived latent embeddings as features for a neural load balancer will enable proactive, topology-aware routing decisions that significantly improve end-to-end service reliability and resource utilization compared to state-of-the-art cloud-native or heuristic schedulers.
proposals.method: We will model the converged infrastructure (compute nodes, links, service chains) as a dynamic attributed graph. A GNN architecture (e.g., GraphSAGE or Temporal GNN) will be trained to generate real-time node/link embeddings capturing topological context and relational dependencies. These embeddings, alongside traditional metrics (load, latency), will form the input state for a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent (e.g., a PPO-based controller) that outputs optimal request routing or resource scheduling actions. The system will be integrated with a cloud-native stack (e.g., Kubernetes with an Envoy proxy) for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel GNN-DRL fusion framework for holistic network-aware load balancing, 2) a methodology for translating dynamic network graphs into actionable embeddings for real-time control, and 3) empirical evidence on performance gains for deterministic services in a simulated converged satellite-terrestrial-vehicular testbed, advancing intelligent resource optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, real-time fusion of GNN-learned topological embeddings with a DRL-based load balancer to achieve *predictive* and *relational* traffic steering, moving beyond reactive per-node optimization to a system-wide, graph-contextual decision-making paradigm for converged networks.

proposals.title: Secure and Deterministic Multi-Agent Resource Orchestration for Heterogeneous Edge-Cloud Systems
proposals.motivation: The convergence of heterogeneous computing resources (CPUs, GPUs, NPUs) at the edge and cloud is critical for latency-sensitive applications like vehicular digital twins and industrial IoT. However, existing deterministic scheduling and resource optimization frameworks largely treat security as a static, binary constraint, failing to co-optimize dynamic security postures (e.g., attestation frequency, encryption overhead) with functional performance and timing guarantees. This gap leaves systems vulnerable to sophisticated threats like side-channel attacks or compromised agents, while undermining the reliability promised by deterministic networking.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling security mechanisms as first-class, schedulable resources within a multi-agent cooperative framework, it is possible to generate orchestration policies that jointly optimize for functional performance, deterministic timing guarantees, and a dynamic, quantifiable security posture resilient to specified threat models.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) architecture where each heterogeneous device is an agent. The state space incorporates functional resource states, temporal constraints from deterministic networking models, and security state vectors (e.g., attestation validity periods, isolation levels). The action space includes both computational task assignments and the selection/activation of security primitives. A constrained optimization problem will be formulated, integrating a security cost model (e.g., overhead of trusted execution environments) with performance and timing objectives, and solved using a multi-agent actor-critic algorithm with safety layers to ensure constraint satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel multi-agent orchestration framework that unifies deterministic scheduling with proactive security resource management, 2) quantitative models for the temporal and resource overhead of dynamic security mechanisms in heterogeneous systems, and 3) new algorithms for generating secure, temporally-guaranteed schedules for use cases like vehicular digital twin networks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is elevating security from a static constraint to a schedulable, multi-dimensional resource that is co-optimized in real-time with performance and timing, a synthesis not addressed by prior work in deterministic networking or secure resource allocation.

proposals.title: Decentralized Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Spectrum and Power Optimization in 6G Vehicular-Satellite Networks
proposals.motivation: The convergence of terrestrial base stations (BSs), autonomous vehicles (AVs), and low-earth orbit (LEO) satellites in 6G vehicular networks creates a highly dynamic and heterogeneous radio environment. Pre-2026 research highlights severe challenges in managing interference and ensuring reliable, low-latency communication under rapidly changing topologies and resource demands. Centralized optimization is infeasible at scale, necessitating a decentralized, intelligent control paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cooperative multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MARL) framework, enhanced with graph attention mechanisms, can enable BSs and AVs to learn decentralized policies for joint spectrum slicing and power control that significantly improve network-wide spectral efficiency and reliability compared to existing heuristic or partially centralized methods.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent actor-critic framework where each BS and AV acts as an independent agent. Agents will utilize a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to process local and neighboring observations (e.g., channel state, interference, queue states) for context-aware decision-making. The training will employ a centralized-training-decentralized-execution (CTDE) paradigm, using a modified Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) algorithm with a novel reward function that balances individual throughput, fairness, and aggregate network interference. The environment will be simulated using an integrated satellite-terrestrial vehicular network simulator.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel decentralized MARL architecture for real-time cross-domain resource management in converged networks. It will provide new insights into the stability and scalability of MARL in large-scale, dynamic wireless systems. The resulting framework and algorithms will serve as a foundational design for autonomous resource orchestration in future 6G integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of graph attention networks within a CTDE-MARL framework specifically for the joint problem of spectrum slicing and power control across the heterogeneous BS-AV-satellite triad, moving beyond single-domain optimization or purely vehicle-to-vehicle scenarios studied in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving and Reliable Resource Orchestration in Vehicular Digital Twin Networks
proposals.motivation: The evolution towards Vehicular Digital Twin Networks (VDTNs) and converged satellite-terrestrial systems creates a critical need for intelligent, real-time resource scheduling across distributed Roadside Units (RSUs). However, centralized learning approaches for optimization policies face significant challenges, including data privacy risks from sharing sensitive vehicular data, high communication overhead, and single points of failure, which compromise system reliability and scalability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning (FL) framework, enhanced with reliability-aware client selection and model aggregation, can enable RSUs to collaboratively learn near-optimal, context-aware resource scheduling policies without raw data exchange, thereby improving system-wide resource efficiency while preserving data privacy and enhancing network resilience.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical FL architecture where RSUs act as clients training local models on their private data (e.g., channel states, task queues). A central orchestrator employs a reliability-aware client selection mechanism, prioritizing RSUs with stable connections and high-quality data. We will design a hybrid optimization problem combining FL model updates (using FedAvg or FedProx) with constrained resource variables (power, spectrum, compute). The Value of Information (VoI) metric will be integrated into the local loss function to prioritize critical control data, and the framework will be validated through simulation using SUMO and NS-3.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel FL-based distributed optimization framework for VDTNs that explicitly balances scheduling performance with data privacy, 2) a reliability-aware FL client selection algorithm to improve convergence and system robustness in dynamic vehicular environments, and 3) quantitative analysis of the trade-offs between learning accuracy, communication overhead, and resource utilization in converged vehicular networks.
proposals.novelty: While FL has been explored for generic wireless networks, its application to the specific, high-reliability context of VDTNs for joint communication and control resource optimization is novel. The integration of VoI-driven local objectives within the FL process to align distributed learning with control system stability goals represents a significant advance over prior art.

proposals.title: Tunable Probabilistic Resilience: A Framework for End-to-End Service Guarantees in Networks with Resource-Bounded Dynamic Adversaries
proposals.motivation: Modern converged and vehicular networks (e.g., Satellite-Terrestrial Vehicular Networks) require robust end-to-end service guarantees for critical applications like digital twins and environment sensing. However, deterministic guarantees are provably impossible under dynamic adversarial faults or sophisticated attacks. Existing resilience models often assume static adversarial strategies or overly pessimistic network models, failing to provide practical, quantifiable assurances for real-world deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally modeling adversarial resources (e.g., computational power, compromised nodes, jamming bandwidth) and network synchrony, we can design protocols that explicitly trade performance metrics (e.g., latency, throughput) for a mathematically proven, tunable probability of correct service delivery.
proposals.method: We will develop a formal adversarial cost model integrating resource bounds and network dynamics. Building on Shen's prior work on deterministic scheduling and VoI-driven optimization, we will design a stochastic optimization framework for joint resource scheduling and path selection. This framework will incorporate lightweight learning-based estimators for adversarial behavior and channel conditions, enabling the dynamic adjustment of a resilience parameter (ρ) to probabilistically bound service violation. Protocol correctness will be analyzed using probabilistic model checking and simulations in NS-3.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for quantifiable, probabilistic resilience in adversarial networks, linking assurance directly to adversarial cost. 2) Practical joint optimization algorithms for scheduling and communication that implement tunable resilience. 3) Analytical and simulation-based validation demonstrating the performance-resilience trade-off in converged vehicular and satellite-terrestrial network scenarios.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond deterministic or static adversarial models by providing a tunable, probabilistic resilience parameter grounded in a formal dynamic adversarial resource model, a significant departure from traditional Byzantine fault tolerance or intrusion tolerance approaches.

proposals.title: Resilient End-to-End Deterministic Scheduling for Converged Networks with Adversarial Nodes
proposals.motivation: Emerging converged networks for industrial IoT and vehicular digital twins require strict end-to-end deterministic guarantees (e.g., bounded latency, zero packet loss). Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Shen's work on deterministic scheduling, assumes cooperative nodes. However, the presence of malicious or faulty nodes—due to cyber-attacks or hardware degradation—can violate these guarantees, threatening safety-critical applications. A framework to maintain determinism under such adversarial conditions is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a Byzantine-resilient consensus mechanism with a time-sensitive networking (TSN) scheduler, it is possible to maintain end-to-end deterministic performance guarantees even when a bounded subset of network nodes exhibits arbitrary (adversarial or faulty) behavior.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer architecture. First, a distributed monitoring layer uses lightweight learning-based anomaly detectors at trusted nodes to identify potential adversarial behavior in the data/control plane. Second, a resilient scheduling layer formulates the resource allocation as a constrained optimization problem, where the objective is to meet deterministic deadlines despite identified faults. We will adapt the 'Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics' optimization method from Dr. Shen's satellite-terrestrial work to dynamically re-route flows and reschedule resources, informed by a Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) consensus protocol among trusted controllers to agree on the network state.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield: 1) a formal model and resilience bound for deterministic networks under adversarial conditions, 2) a practical, co-designed protocol integrating BFT consensus with TSN-like scheduling, and 3) an open-source simulation framework for evaluating deterministic guarantees in faulty settings, advancing the field of resilient mission-critical networking.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes for reliability or resource efficiency, this proposal uniquely addresses the intersection of Byzantine fault tolerance and hard deterministic scheduling, a gap in pre-2026 literature. The co-design of learning-assisted anomaly detection with a robust optimization-based scheduler for converged networks is also novel.

proposals.title: DRL-Based Adaptive Deterministic Scheduling with Graceful Guarantee Degradation for Dynamic Converged Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of time-sensitive applications in converged vehicular, industrial, and satellite-terrestrial networks demands deterministic performance guarantees (e.g., bounded latency, jitter). Existing deterministic scheduling schemes, such as those based on Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) or deterministic networking (DetNet), are largely static or assume fixed flow sets, making them brittle under dynamic flow arrivals/departures. This rigidity leads to inefficient resource use and high flow rejection rates during network congestion, hindering robust service provisioning in truly dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based scheduler can learn to adaptively compute and offer formally guaranteed, degraded service levels (e.g., relaxed latency bounds) when full guarantees are infeasible, thereby increasing overall network utility and flow admission rates while maintaining a contractible performance floor for all admitted flows.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) where the objective is to maximize a utility function of admitted flows subject to hard constraints on their degraded guarantees. A multi-agent DRL framework, potentially leveraging a centralized critic with distributed actors (e.g., MADDPG), will be developed to learn scheduling policies. The state space will incorporate flow characteristics and network conditions, while the action space will include both admission decisions and the selection of a degraded guarantee level from a pre-computed, feasible set derived from schedulability analysis (e.g., using Network Calculus).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel DRL framework for adaptive deterministic scheduling with formal, degradable guarantees, 2) a methodology for real-time computation and offering of contractible service levels under dynamic congestion, and 3) extensive simulation-based validation in converged network scenarios, demonstrating improved admission rates and resource efficiency compared to static or all-or-nothing schedulers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of DRL-based adaptive control with formal schedulability analysis to enable graceful, predictable guarantee degradation—a significant departure from traditional deterministic networking's binary admit/reject decisions and from best-effort adaptive systems lacking formal guarantees.

proposals.title: Risk-Aware Deterministic Scheduling for Converged Networks: A Dynamic Security Posturing Framework
proposals.motivation: The convergence of industrial IoT, vehicular, and satellite-terrestrial networks demands deterministic, low-latency scheduling. However, existing deterministic schedulers, like those in Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) and IETF DetNet, rely on static security models, making them vulnerable to novel, adaptive cyber-physical attacks. This gap is critical as these networks support safety-critical applications where a security breach can cause catastrophic performance failure.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a dynamic, learning-based risk assessment engine directly into the deterministic scheduling loop will enable a resilient network fabric that can proactively optimize the joint objective of performance reliability and security posture, outperforming static, constraint-based security approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer architecture. First, a Dynamic Security Posturing Engine will use lightweight anomaly detection (e.g., autoencoders) on network telemetry and external threat feeds to compute real-time, probabilistic risk scores for flows and nodes. Second, a Risk-Aware Deterministic Scheduler will solve a reformulated optimization problem minimizing a weighted sum of performance loss (e.g., deadline misses) and quantified security risk (e.g., expected impact). We will employ meta-heuristic or safe reinforcement learning algorithms to enable adaptive re-scheduling decisions under evolving threat landscapes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formal framework for quantifying dynamic security risk within deterministic scheduling constraints, 2) a novel joint optimization algorithm for risk-aware resource scheduling, and 3) validated performance-security trade-off models for converged network scenarios, extending the principles of VoI (Value of Information) to security contexts.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats security as a static constraint or an external module, this proposal deeply integrates a dynamic, probabilistic risk model into the core scheduling optimization, enabling the scheduler itself to become an active, adaptive defense mechanism.

proposals.title: Decentralized and Adversarial-Resilient Deterministic Scheduling via Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: The convergence of time-sensitive networks (e.g., for industrial IoT, vehicular networks) demands deterministic scheduling to guarantee bounded latency and reliability. However, centralized schedulers are single points of failure and vulnerable to sophisticated cyber-attacks that can disrupt critical operations. Existing decentralized or learning-based schedulers lack a formal integration of security constraints, leaving them ill-equipped to handle adversarial environments where an attacker can manipulate observations or actions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cooperative multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) framework, with a reward function that explicitly fuses deterministic performance metrics (e.g., makespan, deadline misses) with security risk indicators (e.g., anomaly scores, trust values), can enable a decentralized collective of scheduling agents to autonomously and resiliently optimize resource allocation while mitigating the impact of intrusions and faults.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP). Each scheduling agent (e.g., at a network edge node) will employ an actor-critic DRL architecture, such as MADDPG or QMIX, to learn policies. The core innovation is a composite reward function: R = α*R_perf (e.g., negative weighted sum of latency and jitter) + β*R_sec, where R_sec is derived from a threat model quantifying intrusion risk and fault tolerance. We will train the system in a simulated environment with both stationary and non-stationary attack patterns (e.g., Byzantine agents, perception attacks).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel MADRL framework for security-by-construction deterministic scheduling, 2) a formal methodology to integrate threat models into DRL reward engineering for networked systems, and 3) empirical validation showing emergent security-aware behaviors (like dynamic workload isolation and deceptive resource allocation) that maintain performance within deterministic bounds under attack.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of deterministic scheduling guarantees, a formal security threat model, and cooperative MADRL into a single decentralized framework is novel. Prior work either addresses security orthogonally or uses centralized learning, whereas our approach enables provably robust and adaptively intelligent emergent behavior from a collective of agents.

proposals.title: Secure and Deterministic: A Risk-Aware Scheduling Framework for Converged Time-Sensitive Networks
proposals.motivation: The deterministic scheduling models for converged networks, as proposed in recent literature, guarantee latency and reliability but treat security as a separate, often static, overlay. In pre-2026 industrial IoT and vehicular networks, sophisticated threats like stealthy jamming, time-synchronization attacks, and data integrity breaches can directly violate deterministic service guarantees. A unified optimization framework is urgently needed to co-design security and deterministic performance.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally integrating dynamic security risk metrics and threat models into the deterministic network resource abstraction, a joint optimization algorithm can produce schedules that maintain hard timing guarantees while proactively minimizing vulnerability to targeted attacks.
proposals.method: We will extend the deterministic scheduling abstraction by augmenting resource nodes and links with security-state variables (e.g., trust score, attack exposure). A threat model will define risk propagation, translating security events into latency/jitter penalties. The core is a VoI-driven, two-stage optimization: first, a learning-based risk assessment module evaluates the security-value of scheduling decisions; second, a modified column generation or meta-heuristic algorithm solves the constrained scheduling problem, where security risk is a weighted objective alongside makespan and reliability. This will be validated in a vehicular digital twin network simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, quantifiable framework for risk-aware deterministic scheduling, a set of algorithms for joint security-performance optimization, and concrete performance bounds under adversarial conditions. It directly advances the field of converged network design by moving security from an assumption to a schedulable resource.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that adds security post-scheduling or uses fixed redundancy, this proposal innovates by making security a first-class, dynamic constraint within the core scheduling optimization loop, enabling adaptive trade-offs between deterministic performance and attack resilience.

proposals.title: Physical-Layer Adversarial Stress-Testing for Robustness Certification of End-to-End Deterministic Scheduling in Converged Networks
proposals.motivation: The convergence of TSN, 5G, and satellite networks for mission-critical applications demands provably reliable end-to-end deterministic scheduling (E2eDRS). Pre-2026 validation of such algorithms is largely simulation-based or uses static fault models, failing to capture the complex, non-stationary, and adversarial dynamics of real-world physical infrastructure. This gap risks deploying brittle scheduling solutions that may fail under unforeseen stress, compromising industrial automation, vehicular control, and other latency-sensitive services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subjecting the E2eDRS algorithm to continuous, automated physical-layer adversarial perturbations will reveal critical performance boundaries and failure modes not identified by conventional testing, enabling the generation of a formal robustness certificate that quantifies its operational resilience.
proposals.method: We will deploy the E2eDRS algorithm on a physical converged network testbed integrating TSN switches, 5G URLLC gNodeBs, and software-defined satellite links. A custom Network Perturbation Engine (NPE), leveraging programmable data planes (P4) and software-defined radios, will inject a continuous stream of realistic adversarial conditions: targeted micro-bursts, synchronized link failures, malicious traffic mimicking legitimate flows, and time-varying channel impairments. The system's resilience will be measured via key metrics like schedule recomputation latency, deadline miss ratio under attack, and convergence stability of the underlying optimization (e.g., a learning-augmented heuristic).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) A validated methodology for adversarial resilience testing of network scheduling algorithms on physical testbeds, 2) A robustness certificate for E2eDRS detailing its performance envelopes and identified failure modes, and 3) An open-source NPE framework to enable reproducible stress-testing for the networking community.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond static or simulated validation by implementing continuous, automated adversarial stress-testing at the physical layer of a multi-technology converged testbed, directly linking physical perturbations to control-plane algorithm resilience—a step beyond prior fault injection or digital twin-based analyses.

proposals.title: Testbed-Driven Dynamic Resource Orchestration for AI Agent Services in Multi-Domain Converged Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of AI agent services, from vehicular digital twins to industrial automation, demands stringent reliability and latency guarantees over converged 5G, Wi-Fi, and optical networks. Existing dynamic scaling algorithms are primarily validated in simulation, lacking exposure to the unpredictable hardware bottlenecks, cross-technology protocol interactions, and volatile traffic patterns of real-world converged infrastructures. This gap risks deploying theoretically efficient but practically brittle resource management systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic resource scaling policies, iteratively optimized using real-time performance feedback from a physical multi-technology converged network testbed, will demonstrate significantly higher resilience and reliability for AI agent services compared to policies derived solely from simulated environments.
proposals.method: We will deploy a containerized AI agent service platform on a physical testbed integrating 5G SA core, Wi-Fi 6 access, and optical backhaul. A central orchestrator, implementing a VoI-driven deep reinforcement learning algorithm, will dynamically scale compute and bandwidth resources. The core method is to use the testbed's inherent unpredictability—real hardware bottlenecks, protocol interactions, and injected traffic volatility—as the primary driver for online policy training and evaluation. Performance metrics (latency, packet loss, scaling accuracy) will be fed back to iteratively refine the DRL model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) A novel experimental paradigm that uses physical testbed 'chaos' as a core optimization signal, 2) A set of validated, resilient resource orchestration policies for AI agent services, and 3) An open dataset of converged network performance traces correlated with scaling decisions, bridging the gap between theoretical models and deployable systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the scaling algorithm itself, but in the closed-loop, testbed-in-the-hardware validation methodology, where the complex, non-ideal dynamics of a real converged network are the primary environment for training and proving algorithmic resilience, moving beyond clean-slate simulations.

proposals.title: Meta-Trained Robust Fast-Adaptation for Reliable Network Control in Dynamic Edge Environments
proposals.motivation: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) shows promise for real-time network resource optimization, but its deployment in dynamic edge networks (e.g., vehicular, satellite-terrestrial) is hindered by slow adaptation to new conditions and fragility to out-of-distribution (OOD) perturbations. Pre-2026 meta-RL improves sample efficiency but often overfits to its training task distribution, lacking the robustness required for mission-critical communication and control systems where reliability is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-RL agent, trained on a curriculum of adversarially generated environment variations with an explicit dual-objective loss, will develop policies that achieve both faster convergence in new tasks and superior robustness to OOD disturbances compared to standard meta-RL and robust RL baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop the Meta-RFA framework, building on MAML and adversarial training principles. The core involves a meta-learner and an adversarial environment generator. The generator, using a constrained adversarial network, systematically produces challenging environment parameters (e.g., link delays, node failures, traffic patterns) to expose policy weaknesses. The meta-learner is trained via a modified objective combining the standard meta-loss for fast adaptation and a robustness penalty (e.g., based on the worst-case performance or variance across adversarial perturbations). This creates a curriculum where the agent learns transferable, stable skills.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel meta-RL framework that jointly optimizes for adaptation speed and robustness, 2) a rigorous benchmarking methodology for OOD generalization in network control tasks, and 3) validated policies for simulated vehicular/satellite-terrestrial network scenarios demonstrating reliable performance under unseen disturbances.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of an adversarial curriculum generator into the meta-training loop with a composite loss function, moving beyond sequential application of meta-learning and robustness techniques to co-optimize for fast adaptation and stability against systematic OOD shifts.

proposals.title: Closing the Sim-to-Real Gap: A Multi-Agent DRL Framework for Adaptive Control in Physical Converged Network Testbeds
proposals.motivation: The convergence of heterogeneous networks (e.g., 5G, Wi-Fi, satellite) is critical for future applications like vehicular digital twins and industrial IoT, demanding adaptive, end-to-end resource scheduling. While multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) is a promising solution, policies trained in simulation often fail when deployed due to unmodeled real-world dynamics like hardware jitter, stochastic interference, and protocol-specific latencies. This sim-to-real gap impedes the practical adoption of intelligent network control.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a physical, multi-technology converged network testbed directly into the training and online adaptation loop of a multi-agent DRL system will yield control policies with significantly higher robustness and performance in real-world conditions compared to those trained purely in simulation.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent DRL architecture based on a centralized critic with decentralized actors (e.g., MADDPG) or a fully decentralized approach with communication channels (e.g., CommNet). Agents will initially be pre-trained in a high-fidelity network simulator (e.g., NS-3 with ML co-simulation). The core innovation is the subsequent deployment onto a physical testbed integrating SDN, 5G RAN, and TSN switches, where agents will continue learning via online fine-tuning. The testbed will generate real impairments (packet loss, variable latency) to serve as environmental feedback, enabling policy adaptation. A digital twin of the testbed will facilitate parallel simulation for safer exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce 1) a novel methodology for training and validating network DRL agents that tightly couples simulation with physical testbed interaction, 2) a set of demonstrably robust, adaptive control policies for end-to-end deterministic scheduling across converged networks, and 3) an open dataset and testbed configuration capturing real-world network dynamics for DRL training.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in using DRL for scheduling nor in having a testbed for validation, but in making the physical testbed an integral, interactive component of the DRL training loop itself, enabling real-time policy adaptation to unmodeled hardware and protocol-level stochasticity, which is largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical Graph Neural Network Scheduler for Massive-Scale Deterministic IoT Flows in Converged Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Industrial IoT demands deterministic networking with strict latency and reliability guarantees for thousands of concurrent low-rate, periodic flows. Existing deterministic scheduling algorithms, like those for Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) or IETF DetNet, face severe scalability challenges, leading to intractable computation times or suboptimal resource utilization at such massive scales, hindering the deployment of large-scale smart factories and infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical scheduling framework, decomposing the massive flow problem into manageable sub-problems and leveraging Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to learn flow aggregation and interference patterns, can achieve near-optimal deterministic guarantees with polynomial-time complexity, scaling efficiently to thousands of flows.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier hierarchical scheduler. First, a GNN-based clustering module groups thousands of IoT flows into a manageable number of meta-flows based on spatio-temporal attributes and QoS requirements. Second, a reinforcement learning-enhanced deterministic scheduler allocates resources to these meta-flows within a converged TSN-5G network fabric, treating each cluster's aggregate demand. The scheduler will be trained and evaluated using a digital twin of a large-scale industrial network, integrating real IoT traffic models from pre-2026 datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel hierarchical GNN architecture for massive-scale deterministic flow aggregation, 2) a scalable, learning-based joint scheduling algorithm for converged edge networks, and 3) an open-source digital twin simulation platform for benchmarking massive IoT scheduling, advancing the state-of-the-art in scalable deterministic networking.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of GNNs for intelligent flow structure discovery within a hierarchical optimization framework, specifically designed to overcome the combinatorial complexity barrier of traditional monolithic schedulers for massive IoT, which is an unsolved challenge in pre-2026 deterministic networking research.

proposals.title: Adversarial-Resilient Resource Orchestration for Deterministic Networks: A Learning-Based Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: The convergence of industrial IoT, vehicular networks, and satellite-terrestrial systems demands ultra-reliable, deterministic communication. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Shen's work on deterministic scheduling and vehicular digital twins, primarily addresses stochastic channel variations. However, these systems are increasingly vulnerable to intentional, strategic adversarial attacks like jamming and deceptive interference, which can cripple critical control loops. Existing optimization frameworks lack models for such intelligent, goal-oriented adversaries, creating a significant gap in security-aware reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the adversarial interference as a strategic player in a constrained game and integrating this model into a meta-reinforcement learning (Meta-RL) scheduler, we can co-optimize transmission and resource parameters to maintain deterministic performance bounds (e.g., latency, reliability) even under adaptive attacks.
proposals.method: We will formulate a Stackelberg game where the network controller (leader) allocates resources (power, spectrum, time slots) and the jammer (follower) reacts. The controller's problem will be a constrained optimization to guarantee end-to-end deterministic latency and packet delivery ratio. We will solve this using a Meta-RL agent, specifically a context-augmented Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, trained across diverse adversarial strategies to achieve fast adaptation to unseen attack patterns. The framework will be integrated into a digital twin of a converged satellite-terrestrial vehicular network for validation, extending Dr. Shen's prior architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel game-theoretic model integrating strategic adversarial behavior into deterministic network optimization, 2) a practical Meta-RL-based resource orchestrator capable of maintaining reliability guarantees under adversarial conditions, and 3) an expanded digital twin network simulation platform for evaluating adversarial resilience in converged networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of Stackelberg security game models with meta-reinforcement learning for *deterministic* performance assurance, moving beyond traditional stochastic or worst-case assumptions to handle adaptive, learning-capable adversaries in converged network scheduling.

proposals.title: Federated Learning for Distributed Reliability Optimization in UAV-Assisted Vehicular Networks
proposals.motivation: The convergence of UAVs and vehicular networks for environment sensing and task offloading creates a critical need for reliable, low-latency communication and resource coordination. Centralized optimization requires aggregating sensitive and voluminous user/UAV data, posing privacy risks and significant transmission overhead. A distributed optimization paradigm is urgently needed to enhance system reliability while preserving data locality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning (FL) framework, coordinating UAVs and vehicular users as distributed learners, can jointly optimize communication and computational resource scheduling to maximize end-to-end task reliability without centralizing raw data, outperforming traditional centralized or isolated optimization approaches.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical FL architecture where UAVs act as cluster heads and vehicles as clients. A novel FL aggregation algorithm, incorporating reliability metrics (e.g., packet delivery ratio, computation success probability) into the local loss functions, will be developed. The core optimization problem will jointly consider transmission power, sub-channel allocation, and UAV trajectory, solved iteratively via a federated variant of model-based optimization (e.g., federated alternating direction method of multipliers) combined with deep reinforcement learning for local policy adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel FL framework specifically tailored for reliability-aware optimization in dynamic UAV-vehicle networks. It will provide a concrete methodology for privacy-preserving, distributed resource coordination, advancing the field of converged network management beyond centralized or purely heuristic approaches.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating FL with joint communication-control optimization for reliability, explicitly tackling the trade-off between distributed learning convergence and stringent reliability constraints in a highly mobile, multi-agent aerial-terrestrial network, which remains largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Learning-Based Proactive-Adaptive Scheduling for Bursty IoT Traffic in Converged Satellite-Terrestrial Networks
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of IoT devices for environment sensing and vehicular control generates inherently bursty and unpredictable traffic, challenging traditional network schedulers designed for constant or predictable arrivals. This mismatch leads to severe latency spikes, packet loss, and resource underutilization in converged networks, critically degrading the reliability of applications like vehicular digital twins and industrial automation. Existing deterministic scheduling and resource optimization frameworks lack the agility to handle such stochastic, high-volume bursts effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid scheduler combining a lightweight learning model for short-term burst prediction with a proactive-adaptive optimization layer can dynamically reconfigure network resources to guarantee bounded latency and high reliability for bursty IoT traffic.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture. First, a distributed Transformer-based model at edge aggregators will forecast traffic burst intensity and timing by learning from multi-source IoT data patterns. Second, this forecast will drive a VoI (Value of Information)-aware joint optimization problem, formulated as a Markov Decision Process, to proactively allocate bandwidth and computing slices across satellite and terrestrial segments. The solution will be approximated using a federated Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) algorithm, enabling cooperative resource scheduling without centralizing raw data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel learning-optimization co-design framework for burst-aware scheduling in converged networks, 2) a federated DDPG algorithm for distributed, privacy-preserving resource optimization, and 3) quantitative benchmarks demonstrating improved latency reliability and resource efficiency under synthetic and real-world IoT burst traces compared to state-of-the-art deterministic schedulers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a temporal-sequence learning model specifically for burst prediction with a proactive, VoI-driven resource optimization that adapts network slicing and transmission parameters in anticipation of traffic surges, moving beyond reactive adaptation.

proposals.title: A Mixture-of-Generators Framework for Non-Differentiable Resource Optimization in Converged Vehicular Networks
proposals.motivation: In next-generation vehicular networks, core optimization objectives like end-to-end reliability, scheduling fairness, or control stability are often non-differentiable, discrete, or black-box metrics. Pre-2026 learning-based methods, including policy gradients, struggle with sample efficiency and stability in these high-dimensional, resource-constrained network environments, limiting their practical deployment for real-time resource management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Mixture-of-Generators (MoG) model, trained via a novel evolutionary reinforcement learning scheduler, can effectively approximate and optimize complex, non-differentiable network performance objectives by learning a diverse set of specialized resource allocation policies, outperforming single-policy and differentiable surrogate approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional MoG architecture where each generator outputs a candidate resource schedule (e.g., for communication, sensing, or computation). A scheduler, trained via a quality-diversity evolutionary strategy (inspired by pre-2026 MAP-Elites), will select and combine generators based on direct evaluation of the non-differentiable objective (e.g., a percentile delay or a system-level reliability score). This bypasses the need for differentiable loss proxies. The framework will be integrated into a vehicular digital twin network simulator for iterative policy refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel optimization framework for non-differentiable objectives in networked systems, 2) a concrete algorithm merging MoG with evolutionary search for reliable, sample-efficient policy discovery, and 3) validated use cases in vehicular network scheduling and sensing resource allocation, demonstrating superior performance over gradient-based and single-agent RL baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the typical use of MoG for differentiable likelihoods, instead deploying it as an ensemble of policy generators optimized directly for black-box, non-differentiable network metrics via evolutionary search, a significant departure from pre-2026 gradient-driven learning in networking.

proposals.title: A Mixture-of-Graphs (MoG) Framework for Multi-Domain, Multi-Objective Resource Orchestration in 6G-Enabled Vehicular Digital Twins
proposals.motivation: The evolution towards 6G and pervasive vehicular digital twins (VDTs) creates hyper-complex network environments characterized by heterogeneous resources (sensing, computation, communication), stringent reliability requirements, and multi-stakeholder objectives. Pre-2026 optimization methods often struggle with the combinatorial complexity and dynamic, non-convex nature of joint resource scheduling across converged satellite-terrestrial networks and end-to-end deterministic control loops.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Mixture-of-Graphs (MoG) neural architecture, extended to model multi-domain dependencies and multi-objective trade-offs, can learn near-optimal, adaptive resource orchestration policies that outperform traditional optimization and monolithic graph neural networks in these complex wireless scenarios.
proposals.method: We will extend the MoG paradigm by constructing a heterogeneous super-graph where sub-graphs explicitly model distinct network domains (e.g., vehicle-to-everything, satellite backhaul, edge computing clusters) and resource types. A multi-head attention-based gating network will dynamically weight the contributions of each domain-specific graph neural network (GNN) based on real-time context (e.g., traffic load, channel state, computation urgency). The model will be trained via a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) actor-critic framework with a composite reward function balancing latency, reliability, and resource efficiency, using simulated environments built on SUMO and NS-3.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, scalable MoG-based framework for joint optimization in converged multi-domain networks, 2) new benchmark results for resource scheduling in 6G VDT scenarios, demonstrating quantifiable gains in reliability and efficiency, and 3) a methodology to make complex, coupled optimization problems more tractable via learned, decomposable graph representations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of multi-objective, multi-domain constraints into the MoG learning structure itself, moving beyond single-domain or single-objective applications, and in the fusion of MoG with DRL for adaptive policy generation in a holistic vehicular digital twin network setting.

proposals.title: Secure and Robust Gradient Aggregation for Mobile Gossip Learning: A Federated Learning Transfer Approach
proposals.motivation: Mobile Gossip Learning (MoG) enables decentralized, communication-efficient model training across mobile devices via peer-to-peer exchanges. However, its open, dynamic topology is highly vulnerable to Byzantine attacks and data privacy leakage during local gradient aggregation, which pre-2026 research has not adequately addressed. This security gap hinders the deployment of MoG in critical applications like vehicular networks and IoT, where reliability and data integrity are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that secure and robust gradient aggregation schemes, originally developed for the server-client architecture of Federated Learning (FL), can be effectively adapted and decentralized to fortify MoG against malicious participants and privacy threats, thereby enhancing its convergence reliability without sacrificing communication efficiency.
proposals.method: We will first formalize the threat model for MoG, encompassing Byzantine and inference attacks. Core FL aggregation rules (e.g., Krum, Multi-Krum, and differential privacy mechanisms) will be deconstructed and reformulated for a fully decentralized, asynchronous gossip setting. A novel distributed validation protocol will be designed, leveraging local trust metrics and gradient similarity checks among neighboring nodes to filter malicious updates. The proposed scheme will be integrated into a gossip-based SGD framework and evaluated through simulation in a satellite-terrestrial vehicular network scenario.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, secure, and robust gradient aggregation protocol specifically designed for the MoG paradigm. It will provide theoretical analysis on the convergence bound under attacks and empirical validation of enhanced model accuracy and system resilience. The outcome will be a foundational step towards trustworthy decentralized learning for future mobile networks.
proposals.novelty: While secure aggregation is well-studied in FL, its principled transfer to the fully decentralized, peer-to-peer context of MoG is novel. The key innovation lies in decentralizing the trust anchor (the central server) and designing a distributed consensus mechanism for gradient validation that aligns with the resource and topology constraints of mobile networks.

proposals.title: A Partially Observable Stackelberg Game for Robust Resource Optimization in Vehicular Digital Twin Networks
proposals.motivation: Existing security models in vehicular networks often assume attackers possess perfect knowledge of the defender's resource allocation or scheduling strategy, leading to overly pessimistic or brittle defenses. This assumption is unrealistic in complex, dynamic systems like Vehicular Digital Twin Networks (VDTNs) where defense strategies are internal states. A more realistic model of attacker uncertainty is needed to design robust and efficient resource optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling attackers with partial or noisy observations of the defender's strategy, formulated as a Partially Observable Stackelberg Game (POSG), will yield defense strategies that are more resource-efficient and resilient to realistic adversarial probes than those derived from perfect-information game models.
proposals.method: We will formulate a POSG where the defender (system operator) optimizes communication and computing resource scheduling for VDTN tasks, while the attacker makes imperfect inferences based on observable network side-channels (e.g., delayed control messages, predictable scheduling patterns). The defender's strategy will be optimized using a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm, such as a multi-agent variant of Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), trained against a suite of attacker models with varying observation noise levels. The attacker's belief update will be modeled using a particle filter, capturing the process of noisy strategy estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel game-theoretic framework for security-aware resource optimization under realistic adversarial uncertainty. It will provide concrete algorithms for generating robust defense policies in VDTNs and offer new insights into the value of obfuscation and strategic unpredictability in networked control systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating the concept of partial observability from game theory into the security modeling of VDTNs, moving beyond the standard perfect-information Stackelberg or zero-sum game assumptions prevalent in pre-2026 network security research.

proposals.title: Learning-Based Proactive Defense for Vehicular Networks Under Asymmetric Adversarial Knowledge
proposals.motivation: In emerging vehicular networks (e.g., VDTNs, satellite-terrestrial systems), the defender's traditional information advantage is eroding. Attackers can exploit superior knowledge from compromised sensors, intercepted transmissions, or public data to launch highly targeted, efficient attacks on control and communication systems. This asymmetry fundamentally undermines existing security models that assume defenders have better situational awareness.
proposals.hypothesis: A proactive, learning-based defense strategy that models and anticipates the attacker's decision-making process, despite its information advantage, can invert this asymmetry by forcing the attacker into predictable, suboptimal actions, thereby enhancing overall system reliability.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) framework. First, a digital twin of the vehicular network (integrating communication and control states) will simulate diverse attacker models with varying knowledge superiority. Second, the defender's meta-RL agent will be trained across these varied adversarial scenarios to learn a robust policy that optimizes joint communication resource scheduling and control command adaptation. This policy will be validated in a cooperative satellite-terrestrial vehicular network simulator, using metrics for safety, latency, and resource efficiency under attack.
