proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formal model for adversarial knowledge asymmetry in converged vehicular control-communication systems, 2) a novel meta-RL-based defense algorithm that proactively mitigates threats from better-informed attackers, and 3) quantitative analysis of the reliability-resource trade-off in inverted-information scenarios.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on cooperative scheduling or VoI-driven optimization which assumes benevolent or equally-informed agents, this proposal explicitly inverts the core security assumption, treating the attacker's superior knowledge as a first-class constraint in the joint optimization problem.

proposals.title: Robustness of Deterministic Network Schedulers Against Adversarial Reinforcement Learning Attacks
proposals.motivation: Emerging deterministic networking (e.g., IETF DetNet, IEEE TSN) is critical for time-sensitive applications in vehicular and industrial IoT. However, its scheduling and resource optimization mechanisms, often based on learning for adaptability, are vulnerable to intelligent adversaries. Pre-2026 research on network security rarely evaluates defenses against adaptive, learning-based attackers who can probe and exploit system dynamics, creating a critical gap in reliability assurance for converged cyber-physical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deterministic schedulers optimized for traditional disturbance models will exhibit significant performance degradation under adversarial reinforcement learning (ARL) attacks, and that a co-designed defense integrating moving target defense (MTD) with robust adversarial training can maintain schedulability and end-to-end latency guarantees.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-player Markov game between an ARL attacker (modeled as a PPO agent) and a defender managing a deterministic scheduler in a vehicular digital twin network. The attacker's action space targets scheduling parameters and channel sensing reports. The defender will employ a hybrid method: 1) a primary scheduler trained via adversarial RL to be robust, and 2) a meta-layer applying MTD by dynamically altering resource virtualization and scheduling seed parameters. Evaluation will use extended satellite-terrestrial network simulators, measuring metrics like deadline miss ratio and control stability under attack.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind framework for stress-testing deterministic network resource management against adaptive adversaries. It will yield concrete robustness benchmarks and a co-design methodology for secure scheduling, directly enhancing the reliability of proposed converged networks for critical infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work applies learning for optimization or static threat models, this proposal uniquely frames the network scheduling problem as a dynamic game against a learning adversary, integrating adversarial machine learning directly into the core control loop of deterministic transmission.

proposals.title: Robust Optimization for Adversarially Resilient Resource Scheduling in Converged Satellite-Terrestrial Vehicular Networks
proposals.motivation: The increasing reliance on machine learning for intelligent resource scheduling in converged vehicular networks introduces critical vulnerabilities to adversarial attacks. Pre-2026 research by Shen et al. has optimized scheduling for reliability and efficiency, but these learned models can be compromised by subtle input perturbations, threatening the safety and determinism of time-sensitive vehicular applications. A defense mechanism is urgently needed to ensure robust network operation in adversarial environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating adversarial training and distributionally robust optimization (DRO) into the resource scheduling framework will significantly enhance its resilience against adversarial perturbations without compromising baseline performance metrics like latency and reliability.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage adversarial defense framework. First, we will augment the training data for the scheduling model (e.g., a graph neural network or deep Q-network) with adversarial examples generated via Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) attacks on network state inputs. Second, we will formulate a DRO-based scheduling objective that minimizes worst-case performance over a Wasserstein ambiguity set of perturbed channel states and traffic distributions, directly embedding robustness into the optimization problem. This combined approach will be evaluated within a digital twin of a satellite-terrestrial vehicular network.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, adversarially robust resource scheduler for converged vehicular networks, advancing the field of secure network automation. It will provide both empirical validation and a theoretical DRO formulation for robustness, contributing new benchmarks for evaluating scheduling reliability under attack.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial defenses exist in computer vision, their application to the joint optimization of communication and control resources in dynamic, multi-layer vehicular networks is novel. This work uniquely merges adversarial ML with distributionally robust optimization specifically for the network scheduling domain pioneered by Shen's group.

proposals.title: Online Learning for Adaptive Defense: A Contextual Bandit Framework for Dynamic Resource Scheduling in Vehicular Networks
proposals.motivation: Emerging vehicular networks, including digital twin and satellite-terrestrial systems, face dynamic and adversarial environments where communication and sensing resources are perpetually contested. Pre-2026 research by Shen's group highlights the need for deterministic scheduling and joint control-communication optimization, but static or model-based strategies struggle with unknown and non-stationary attack patterns or jamming behaviors. An online, data-driven approach to defense strategy adaptation is critical for maintaining reliability and Quality of Service in real-time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a contextual multi-armed bandit (MAB) framework, integrating real-time network state and threat indicators, can learn optimal defense actions (e.g., channel hopping, power adjustment, task offloading) to outperform static scheduling and traditional optimization under persistent, evolving threats.
proposals.method: We propose a contextual combinatorial bandit model where the context vector includes channel state, vehicle density, and anomaly detection metrics. The 'arms' are composite defense actions over the resource dimensions identified in Shen's prior work (e.g., transmission slot, sensing node selection, compute offload target). We will develop a Thompson Sampling or Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) variant that efficiently handles the combinatorial action space. This online learner will be integrated into a distributed scheduler at the road-side unit or satellite gateway, enabling strategy updates based on immediate reward signals like packet delivery ratio and sensing accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel online learning framework for adaptive security and resource management in converged vehicular networks. It will provide theoretical analysis on the regret bound of the proposed algorithm and demonstrate, via simulation using pre-2026 datasets like Veins, significant improvements in reliability and resource efficiency under jamming and spoofing attacks compared to baseline optimization methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of contextual bandits with the specific combinatorial resource scheduling problem for vehicular network defense, moving beyond offline optimization or supervised learning to a real-time, feedback-driven adaptation paradigm with provable learning guarantees in this domain.

proposals.title: Differential Privacy Inference Attacks and Robust Defense in Resource-Constrained Vehicular Digital Twin Networks
proposals.motivation: Vehicular Digital Twin (VDT) networks, which rely on continuous data aggregation from vehicles for real-time synchronization, increasingly employ differential privacy (DP) to protect individual vehicle data. However, pre-2026 research primarily focuses on defending against integrity attacks like data poisoning, while the vulnerability of DP mechanisms to sophisticated inference attacks aimed at privacy breaches in dynamic, resource-constrained networks is under-explored. This gap is critical as successful privacy inference can compromise user location and behavior patterns, eroding trust in VDT systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a VDT network with constrained communication and computing resources, an adversary can design a model-based inference attack that strategically exploits the network's resource optimization and scheduling patterns to significantly degrade the effective privacy guarantee (epsilon) of a standard DP mechanism. Furthermore, a joint optimization framework for communication scheduling and adaptive DP parameters can mitigate this attack while maintaining system reliability.
proposals.method: We will first model the VDT network as a time-varying graph and formalize a Value-of-Information (VoI)-driven resource scheduling problem. An adversarial agent, equipped with a deep reinforcement learning model, will learn to infer private attributes by observing the scheduled transmission patterns and the perturbed aggregated data. The attack's efficacy will be quantified by the inferred epsilon. Subsequently, we will develop a cooperative defense algorithm that jointly optimizes transmission scheduling and per-vehicle adaptive DP noise injection, formulated as a constrained optimization problem to balance privacy, communication reliability, and twin model accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel threat model and evaluation framework for inference attacks against DP in dynamic VDT networks, 2) a robust, joint control-communication-privacy optimization scheme that enhances privacy under resource constraints. It will advance the understanding of privacy-security trade-offs beyond traditional integrity threats.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the adversarial focus from data integrity to privacy inference within a resource-optimized network control loop, and in proposing a co-designed mitigation that integrates DP parameters directly into the network's VoI-based scheduling optimization, a approach not seen in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimization of Differential Privacy Noise for Inherent Robustness Against Data Poisoning in Networked Learning Systems
proposals.motivation: Data poisoning attacks pose a severe threat to the reliability of distributed learning systems, such as those in vehicular and satellite-terrestrial networks. While Differential Privacy (DP) is a standard privacy tool, its noise is typically calibrated for privacy-utility trade-offs, not for adversarial robustness. This work investigates whether DP's inherent noise injection can be strategically designed to inherently resist poisoning, addressing a critical security gap in pre-2026 network optimization and learning paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating an inverse optimization problem, we can derive a DP noise distribution that optimally disrupts an attacker's ability to craft effective poisoning samples, thereby enhancing model robustness without solely relying on post-hoc detection or filtering mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will formulate a Stackelberg game between a defender (optimizing noise) and an attacker (crafting poison data). The defender's objective is to maximize the attacker's expected error in achieving a target model perturbation, subject to DP (ε,δ)-constraints and learning convergence guarantees. We will employ adversarial risk analysis and bi-level optimization, solving the inner loop with projected gradient ascent for attack generation and the outer loop using reinforcement learning or evolutionary strategies to search the noise parameter space (e.g., tailoring the scale and shape of Laplace or Gaussian mechanisms). The framework will be validated in simulated vehicular digital twin and satellite-terrestrial network environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled methodology for co-designing privacy and robustness in networked learning systems. It will provide concrete guidelines for selecting DP noise parameters that inherently harden systems against poisoning, advancing beyond traditional additive-noise DP. The resulting joint optimization framework will enhance the reliability and security of resource-constrained, distributed intelligence applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating DP noise not just as a privacy-preserving additive, but as a tunable defensive parameter to be optimized directly against an adversarial objective—an 'inverse' perspective on DP mechanism design. This integrates adversarial robustness into the core DP optimization problem, a non-obvious fusion of security and privacy goals pre-2026.

proposals.title: Resilient Cooperative Edge Intelligence via Value-of-Information and Graph Learning for Unstable Adversarial Networks
proposals.motivation: Emerging cooperative edge intelligence paradigms, such as federated learning and vehicular digital twins, critically assume reliable, high-bandwidth links. However, in real-world scenarios like satellite-terrestrial vehicular networks or contested environments, links are highly unstable, bandwidth-constrained, and potentially adversarial. This mismatch threatens the reliability and determinism promised by next-generation converged networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint framework integrating dynamic Value-of-Information (VoI) assessment with robust graph neural network-based scheduling can maintain critical application performance and learning convergence, even under severe link instability and selective adversarial interference.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier resilient scheduler. First, a VoI-driven meta-controller will dynamically prioritize sub-tasks and data flows based on their real-time impact on global objective (e.g., model accuracy, control stability) under observed network conditions. Second, a Graph Attention Network (GAT)-based executor will learn to map these priorities onto actual transmission schedules and resource blocks, trained via a Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) agent that experiences simulated link failures, delays, and jamming attacks. The optimization problem will jointly minimize task completion time and maximize VoI throughput.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel VoI-graph learning framework for resilient edge cooperation, 2) a new problem formulation and benchmark for resource scheduling under adversarial network conditions, and 3) quantitative analysis of the trade-offs between learning reliability, communication overhead, and security in unstable environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of VoI-based semantic importance with graph-based relational learning specifically for adversarial edge networks, moving beyond traditional optimization that assumes benign, predictable links.

proposals.title: Secure and Reliable Federated Aggregation for Resource-Constrained Vehicular Digital Twin Networks
proposals.motivation: The deployment of Federated Learning (FL) in dynamic, large-scale networks like Vehicular Digital Twin Networks (VDTNs) faces critical challenges. Intermediate model updates transmitted between vehicles and edge servers are vulnerable to privacy inference attacks and Byzantine faults, while the highly mobile and resource-constrained environment demands communication-efficient and reliable aggregation protocols. Pre-2026 research by Shen's group on VDTN optimization and deterministic scheduling highlights this tension between intelligent control, communication reliability, and resource constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a secure multi-party computation (MPC)-based aggregation protocol with a reliability-aware client selection mechanism will significantly enhance the privacy and Byzantine robustness of FL in VDTNs, without prohibitive communication overhead, leading to more accurate and trustworthy digital twin models.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tiered aggregation framework for a VDTN. First, a lightweight MPC protocol (e.g., based on additive secret sharing) will be applied among a committee of edge servers to perform secure aggregation, preventing any single server from accessing raw model updates. Second, at the network edge, we will design a joint optimization problem for client (vehicle) selection, balancing model freshness (based on vehicular mobility patterns), historical reliability scores, and wireless resource allocation (inspired by Shen's prior work on cooperative resource scheduling). The core FL algorithm will integrate these components, iteratively solving the client selection and resource optimization problem before applying the secure aggregation protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, holistic FL framework tailored for VDTNs that jointly addresses privacy, reliability, and system efficiency. It will advance the field by providing a concrete method to realize VoI-driven, secure collaborative learning in mobile cyber-physical systems. The proposed algorithms will be validated through simulations incorporating realistic vehicular network traces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of cryptographic secure aggregation with a dynamic, reliability-and-mobility-aware client selection mechanism specifically for the VDTN context, moving beyond applying generic FL or security methods in isolation. This directly extends Shen's recent work on VDTN joint optimization by injecting a core privacy-preserving learning primitive.

proposals.title: A Robustness-Aware Resource Optimization Framework for Platooning in Vehicular Digital Twin Networks Under Adversarial and Faulty Conditions
proposals.motivation: Connected and autonomous vehicle platooning relies on high-fidelity data exchange and coordinated control, making it vulnerable to both malicious attacks (e.g., data injection) and random sensor/actuator faults. Pre-2026 research on vehicular digital twin networks and deterministic scheduling has primarily focused on performance optimization under ideal conditions, leaving a critical gap in understanding and mitigating the impact of compromised or malfunctioning vehicles on platoon safety and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a real-time anomaly detection module with a Value-of-Information (VoI)-driven, resilient resource scheduling mechanism, the platooning system's control performance and communication reliability can be maintained within safe bounds even in the presence of adversarial data or faulty vehicle behavior.
proposals.method: We will extend a Vehicular Digital Twin Network architecture with a two-layer defense. First, a lightweight, federated learning-based anomaly detector will be deployed at edge nodes to identify malicious data patterns and faulty state reports from individual vehicles. Second, the detection confidence will be fed into a modified VoI-driven joint optimization problem, formulated as a constrained Markov Decision Process. This will dynamically re-allocate communication resources (e.g., in a satellite-terrestrial network) and adjust control gains, isolating untrusted data and prioritizing reliable vehicles to preserve platoon string stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel integrated framework for robustness-aware optimization in vehicular platooning, 2) a concrete methodology to quantify and mitigate the impact of adversarial/faulty agents on end-to-end system performance, and 3) new insights into the trade-offs between communication resource efficiency, control resilience, and security in converged networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of security-aware anomaly detection and resilient, VoI-based resource scheduling specifically for the platooning use-case within a Digital Twin framework, moving beyond traditional perimeter security or post-hoc fault tolerance.

proposals.title: Joint Communication, Sensing, and Computation Resource Optimization for Digital Twin Networks under Server-Side Computational Constraints
proposals.motivation: Current Digital Twin (DT) network optimization frameworks, particularly for vehicular and satellite-terrestrial networks, often assume infinite or unconstrained computing resources at the DT server. This assumption is unrealistic for practical deployments where server CPU cycles, memory, and processing latency are finite and shared among numerous DT instances. Ignoring these constraints can lead to infeasible control policies, synchronization failures, and degraded Quality-of-Twin (QoT) in latency-critical applications like vehicular digital twins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the DT server's computational resource constraints within a joint optimization framework will yield more realistic, reliable, and efficient scheduling and resource allocation policies, significantly improving system-wide performance compared to models that assume infinite computing resources.
proposals.method: We will formulate a joint optimization problem integrating communication (bandwidth, power), sensing (task scheduling, sensor selection), and computation (server CPU cycles, queueing delay) resources. The problem will be modeled as a constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) to handle dynamic network states. We will develop a distributed learning-based algorithm, building on Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) or Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning, enhanced with Lagrangian multipliers to handle the computational constraints. The algorithm will be evaluated using a DT network simulator integrating SUMO for vehicular traffic and NS-3 for network emulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel optimization framework that realistically incorporates server-side computational bottlenecks in DT networks. It will deliver new constraint-aware scheduling algorithms and establish key trade-off analyses between communication reliability, sensing accuracy, and computational load, offering practical design guidelines for DT system architects.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Shen et al. optimizes communication and control, and some studies address edge computation, the explicit joint modeling of server-side computational resources as a core constraint within a DT network optimization problem is a novel and necessary advancement for the field's transition to practical systems.

proposals.title: Black-Box Adversarial Attack Generation for Network Intrusion Detection Systems via Query-Efficient Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) increasingly employ deep learning models for their high accuracy. However, existing adversarial attack research often assumes white-box access to the target model, which is unrealistic for real-world attackers. Evaluating NIDS robustness under practical black-box or gray-box threat models, where the attacker lacks full model knowledge, is a critical and underexplored security problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating the attack as a query-efficient, meta-reinforcement learning problem, an adversary can reliably generate successful adversarial network traffic perturbations without knowledge of the target NIDS's internal architecture or training data.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage meta-learning framework. First, we train a meta-attacker policy on a diverse ensemble of surrogate NIDS models (e.g., CNNs, RNNs, GNNs) using a reinforcement learning objective that maximizes evasion. Second, for a black-box target, the meta-policy is fine-tuned with a limited number of queries, where the feedback is solely the target's classification label. The attack actions will perturb protocol fields and timing features within semantically-constrained bounds to maintain functional network flows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, practical methodology for black-box adversarial evaluation of NIDS, moving beyond theoretical white-box assumptions. It will contribute a novel meta-reinforcement learning attack framework that balances query efficiency and attack success rate, offering new insights into the vulnerability landscape of learning-based network security.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating meta-learning with constrained reinforcement learning specifically for the NIDS domain, enabling efficient black-box attack adaptation without prior model knowledge, which is distinct from gradient-based white-box methods or simple transfer attacks.

proposals.title: Adversarial Non-Reversible Traffic Obfuscation: A New Threat to Learning-Based Network Security and Resource Optimization
proposals.motivation: Modern network management, from deterministic scheduling to vehicular digital twins, increasingly relies on machine learning models that analyze traffic representations for optimization and security. These models assume that traffic patterns, while complex, are fundamentally reversible representations of underlying applications. This creates a critical vulnerability: if an adversary can craft traffic that is functionally malicious (e.g., exfiltrating data, disrupting control) but is represented in a way that is non-reversible to its benign source, it can evade detection while maintaining its payload. This threat is underexplored in pre-2026 literature, which focuses on reversible adversarial examples or obvious anomalies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating a constrained optimization problem that jointly preserves malicious application-layer functionality while maximizing the non-reversibility of its traffic representation (e.g., through sequence, timing, and protocol feature manipulation), an attacker can create a novel class of adversarial network flows that reliably bypass state-of-the-art learning-based intrusion detection and resource schedulers.
proposals.method: We will formulate a two-stage framework. First, we design a generative adversarial network (GAN) where the generator produces subtle, non-reversible packet sequence and timing perturbations, and the discriminator is a pre-trained traffic classifier (e.g., a CNN-LSTM model). Second, we integrate a functional constraint module, inspired by control-communication co-design principles, that ensures the perturbed traffic flow still satisfies the communication semantics required for the malicious payload (e.g., successful TCP session for data exfiltration). The attack's efficacy will be evaluated against Shen's recent work on deterministic scheduling and vehicular digital twin networks using metrics of evasion rate, functional success rate, and reversibility distance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a formal threat model and taxonomy for non-reversible adversarial traffic in learning-based networks, 2) a novel attack generation algorithm that explicitly decouples malicious functionality from traffic representation, and 3) a benchmark and analysis of vulnerabilities in state-of-the-art network optimization and security systems, providing foundational knowledge for designing more robust, representation-aware defenses.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior adversarial attacks in networking that create reversible perturbations or noise, this work fundamentally challenges the core assumption of reversibility between traffic representation and application intent, creating a stealthier attack paradigm that is uniquely potent against learning-based network systems.

proposals.title: Adversarial Traffic Generation via Functionality-Aware Constraint Relaxation for Evading Deterministic Network Schedulers
proposals.motivation: Emerging deterministic networks (e.g., IETF DetNet, 5G/6G TSN) use intelligent scheduling to guarantee ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC). This creates a robust defense against traditional adversarial traffic patterns. However, these schedulers rely on strict traffic models; an adversary that can strategically violate its declared model in a controlled, non-critical way may evade detection while still achieving its primary disruptive goal. This exposes a critical vulnerability in next-generation network security.
proposals.hypothesis: By relaxing the constraint of preserving all original traffic functionality and strategically sacrificing non-critical, low-Value-of-Information (VoI) features, an adversarial agent can generate traffic patterns that significantly increase evasion success against learning-based deterministic schedulers without compromising the core adversarial objective.
proposals.method: We formulate this as a constrained optimization problem where the reward balances evasion success against the preservation of critical functionality, quantified by a VoI metric. A deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent, using a modified Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, will be trained to explore the space of permissible traffic deformations (e.g., jitter injection, minor period alterations, packet size changes). The agent interacts with a digital twin of a converged satellite-terrestrial vehicular network, which hosts a target deterministic scheduler (e.g., a GNN-based scheduler) to be evaded. The constraint relaxation is explicitly encoded in the action space and reward function.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for generating functionality-aware adversarial traffic against deterministic networks. 2) A quantitative analysis of the trade-off between evasion rate and preserved functionality, introducing a new metric for adversarial resilience. 3) Practical insights for hardening learning-based network schedulers against this new class of semantic-aware attacks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior adversarial network attacks that seek to mimic legitimate traffic or cause blind disruption, this work strategically trades off non-essential functionality for evasion, a concept unexplored in pre-2026 adversarial machine learning for networking. It uniquely integrates VoI-driven control theory with adversarial DRL in a digital twin testbed.

proposals.title: A Learning-Based Framework for Efficient Adversarial Traffic Generation in Deterministic Networks
proposals.motivation: The PATG (Policy-Aware Traffic Generation) method is crucial for proactively evaluating the security and reliability of deterministic networks, such as those envisioned for 6G and industrial IoT. However, its computational intensity and slow speed hinder rapid attack iteration and real-time vulnerability assessment, limiting its practical deployment for dynamic network defense. Accelerating PATG is essential for enabling fast, adaptive security testing in next-generation converged communication systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a lightweight surrogate model and a gradient-based optimization strategy, the adversarial traffic generation process can be significantly accelerated without compromising the attack's success rate or stealth. This will enable near-real-time generation of effective adversarial traffic patterns.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a compact Graph Neural Network (GNN) surrogate model will be trained to approximate the complex scheduling policy of the target deterministic network, replacing expensive simulations. Second, we will formulate the traffic generation as a constrained optimization problem and solve it using a projected gradient ascent method, leveraging gradients from the differentiable surrogate to rapidly converge to adversarial samples. The framework will be specifically tailored for time-sensitive network (TSN) and IETF Deterministic Networking (DetNet) scheduling models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, computationally efficient adversarial traffic generation tool that drastically reduces iteration time from hours to minutes. It will contribute a methodology for applying gradient-based attacks to network scheduling policies, advancing the field of proactive network security assessment. The resulting framework will be a key enabler for continuous, automated resilience testing in vehicular digital twin and satellite-terrestrial networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a learnable, policy-aware surrogate model with gradient-based optimization specifically for network flow adversarial attacks, a departure from prior slow, simulation-heavy or heuristic-based PATG approaches. This directly addresses the speed bottleneck for security evaluation in deterministic networking, a critical pre-2026 research frontier.

proposals.title: Diffusion-Driven Adversarial Traffic Generation for Robust Network Scheduler Training
proposals.motivation: The increasing demand for deterministic, low-latency services in converged networks requires intelligent schedulers trained on diverse traffic scenarios. Existing adversarial traffic generation methods, often based on GANs, suffer from mode collapse and limited sample diversity, leading to schedulers that are brittle in real-world deployments. This gap hinders the reliability of next-generation networks for critical applications like vehicular digital twins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that diffusion probabilistic models, by learning a more complete data distribution through a gradual denoising process, can generate a broader and more realistic spectrum of adversarial network traffic patterns than prior generative models, thereby enabling the training of more robust and generalizable network schedulers.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) to treat multi-dimensional time-series network traffic matrices (e.g., source-destination, packet size, inter-arrival time) as its 'image' domain. The forward process will add Gaussian noise to real traffic traces, and the reverse process will be trained to reconstruct them. A conditional diffusion model will be developed, using network state parameters (e.g., topology, load) as conditioning inputs to guide the generation of context-aware adversarial samples. These generated samples will be used to adversarially train a deep reinforcement learning-based scheduler, iteratively improving both the generator's realism and the scheduler's resilience.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel diffusion-based framework for high-fidelity, diverse network traffic generation, 2) a new adversarial training paradigm for network control algorithms that enhances robustness against rare but disruptive traffic patterns, and 3) empirical validation in satellite-terrestrial vehicular network and converged network testbeds, demonstrating improved scheduler reliability under stress.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are established in image synthesis, their application to structured, time-series network traffic for adversarial robustness is novel. This approach fundamentally differs from GAN-based methods by offering stable training and superior coverage of the data distribution's tails, where critical adversarial examples reside.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Adaptive Defense in Deterministic Converged Networks
proposals.motivation: Emerging converged networks (e.g., vehicular, satellite-terrestrial) require end-to-end deterministic and reliable transmission for critical control tasks. Pre-2026, these networks face rapidly evolving, unknown cyber-attacks (e.g., adversarial perturbations, novel jamming patterns). Traditional learning-based defenses require extensive retraining data and time, failing to adapt quickly to new threats, which compromises network reliability and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework, trained on a distribution of known attack scenarios, can enable a network defense model to rapidly adapt to novel, unseen attack patterns using only a few-shot samples, thereby maintaining high reliability with minimal performance degradation.
proposals.method: We propose a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework integrated into a network controller. The inner-loop optimization will quickly adapt a base anomaly detection model (e.g., a Graph Neural Network for network state) using few-shot samples of a new attack. The outer-loop will meta-optimize the model's initial parameters across a diverse set of simulated attack tasks. This will be tested within a time-sensitive networking (TSN) and vehicular digital twin simulation, optimizing the joint control-communication resource scheduling policy under adaptive adversarial conditions.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel meta-learning-based defense mechanism that enhances the adaptability and resilience of deterministic converged networks. 2) A joint optimization framework for network resource scheduling and security policy that dynamically reallocates resources (e.g., bandwidth, compute) based on the meta-learned defense model's requirements.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored in computer vision, its application for fast adaptive security in deterministic, resource-constrained converged networks is novel. This work uniquely integrates meta-learning with network control and resource optimization loops, moving beyond static intrusion detection systems.

proposals.title: Adversarial Swarm Traffic Generation: A Learning-Based Attack on Coordinated IIoT Botnet Detection
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) devices has created large-scale, coordinated attack surfaces. Pre-2026 botnet detection systems increasingly rely on machine learning to identify anomalous traffic patterns from single devices or simple clusters, but they are vulnerable to sophisticated, swarm-level adversarial patterns that mimic legitimate coordination. This gap threatens the reliability of critical infrastructure networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the multi-device resource scheduling and communication protocols of IIoT swarms, an adversarial learning agent can generate covert, coordinated traffic patterns that evade state-of-the-art botnet detection while achieving malicious objectives.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) optimization, where each agent controls a compromised IIoT device. The adversarial policy will be trained against a surrogate detector (e.g., a Graph Neural Network-based detector) to minimize detection probability. The method will incorporate constraints from real IIoT transmission protocols and resource scheduling models (inspired by Shen's work on deterministic scheduling and vehicular network optimization) to ensure traffic pattern feasibility. The training will use a centralized critic with decentralized actors to enable covert, distributed execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel adversarial attack framework for coordinated multi-device botnets, 2) a publicly available dataset of adversarial IIoT swarm traffic patterns for benchmarking, and 3) new insights into the vulnerabilities of learning-based network intrusion detection systems at the swarm level, guiding the development of more robust defenses.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing adversarial attacks focused on single endpoints or simple packet manipulation, this work explicitly attacks the *coordinated behavior* and *temporal resource scheduling patterns* of a device swarm, a dimension largely unexplored in pre-2026 network security research.

proposals.title: PATGen-Enhanced Adversarial Training for Robust Network Intrusion Detection in Converged Edge Networks
proposals.motivation: Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) based on machine learning are vulnerable to adversarial attacks that manipulate traffic features to evade detection. This is a critical threat to the security of converged edge networks, such as vehicular and satellite-terrestrial systems, where reliable and deterministic communication is paramount. Existing adversarial training methods often rely on simple gradient-based perturbations, which may not generate realistic, protocol-aware attack traffic, limiting the robustness of hardened models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a Protocol-Aware Traffic Generator (PATG) to synthesize realistic, feature-space adversarial examples for training will produce NIDS models with significantly higher robustness against sophisticated, protocol-compliant evasion attacks, compared to models trained with standard adversarial examples.
proposals.method: We will first design a PATG framework, informed by pre-2026 generative models like GANs or flow-based models, to learn the joint distribution of legitimate network traffic features and protocol constraints. This generator will be integrated into an adversarial training loop where, for each mini-batch, PATG produces plausible adversarial variants. We will formulate a min-max optimization problem where the NIDS model (a deep neural network) is trained to minimize loss on both clean and PATG-generated adversarial data, hardening it against a diverse threat landscape. The approach will be evaluated on vehicular and satellite-terrestrial network traffic datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel adversarial training framework that integrates domain-specific (protocol-aware) traffic generation to improve NIDS robustness. It will provide new benchmarks for evaluating NIDS resilience in converged networks and advance the methodology for building secure, learning-based network management systems, a core theme in Prof. Shen's research on reliable resource optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of a generative, protocol-aware model (PATG) to create realistic adversarial training data for NIDS, moving beyond generic perturbation methods. This bridges the gap between adversarial machine learning and domain-specific network traffic synthesis for security hardening.

ideas.title: Optimize adversarial traffic generation for speed
ideas.description: Improve the computational efficiency and speed of the PATG method for faster attack iteration.
ideas.sourceTitle: Modeling Realistic Adversarial Traffic Against Deep-Learning-Based Intrusion Detection System in Industrial IoT

ideas.title: Decentralized Adversarial-Resilient Scheduling via Multi-Agent DRL
ideas.description: This research synthesizes deterministic scheduling with security constraints and multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) to create a novel framework for decentralized, adversarial-resilient resource management. The core idea is to model scheduling agents as cooperative DRL agents whose reward function integrates both traditional performance objectives (e.g., makespan, latency) and security metrics derived from a formal threat model (e.g., intrusion risk, fault tolerance). This enables the emergent, coordinated behavior of the agent collective to not only optimize for efficiency but also proactively enact security-aware decisions—like dynamic workload isolation, deceptive resource allocation, or adaptive checkpointing—in response to perceived threats, all without a vulnerable central controller. The result is a scheduling paradigm that is both provably robust within a deterministic envelope and adaptively intelligent against sophisticated, non-stationary attacks.

ideas.title: Apply adversarial machine learning defenses

ideas.title: Quantifiable Probabilistic Resilience for Networks with Bounded Dynamic Adversaries
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for providing quantifiable, probabilistic end-to-end service guarantees in networks containing malicious or faulty nodes, without relying on unrealistic assumptions of a static or bounded adversarial strategy. The core innovation is a tunable resilience parameter that allows system designers to explicitly trade performance metrics for a mathematically proven probability of correctness, given a formal model of adversarial resources (e.g., bandwidth, computational power, number of nodes) and network synchrony. The approach yields protocols and analysis techniques that deliver robust, practical assurances where deterministic guarantees are provably impossible, directly linking system resilience to measurable adversarial cost.

ideas.title: Adapting Differential Privacy from Census Data for Federated Agent Training
ideas.description: This idea expands the original federated learning concept for decentralized agent training by drawing an analogy from the field of statistical data privacy, specifically the methods used by national census bureaus. The analogous solution is the application of differential privacy to protect individual respondents in published census statistics. We adapt this by integrating a differential privacy framework, such as DP-FedAvg, into the federated learning cycle for collaborative agent improvement. In this adapted system, each agent locally trains its model, then applies noise addition and gradient clipping to its model updates before sharing them for secure aggregation. This creates a robust, privacy-preserving training protocol where agents contribute to a collectively improved global model without exposing their unique experiences or sensitive local data, analogous to how census data reveals population trends without compromising individual citizen privacy.

ideas.title: Test defense under adaptive attack strategies

ideas.title: Multi-Agent DRL for Real-World, Adaptive Control in Converged Network Testbeds
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the development of multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for distributed control with its rigorous evaluation on a physical, multi-technology converged network testbed. The core innovation is to use the testbed not merely as a final validation platform, but as an integral part of the training and adaptation loop, where agents learn from and respond to real hardware latencies, stochastic link impairments, and protocol interoperability challenges. This synergy allows for the co-design of DRL algorithms and network deployment strategies, producing control policies that are inherently robust to real-world noise and heterogeneity. The outcome is a demonstrated, practical framework for adaptive, end-to-end scheduling that bridges the sim-to-real gap in networked systems.

ideas.title: Apply meta-learning for adaptive defense
ideas.description: Use meta-learning from few-shot learning to quickly adapt defenses to new attack patterns.
ideas.sourceTitle: Modeling Realistic Adversarial Traffic Against Deep-Learning-Based Intrusion Detection System in Industrial IoT

ideas.title: Real-Time, Physical Testbed Validation of Dynamic Resource Scaling for AI Agent Services in Converged Networks
ideas.description: This research fuses physical testbed validation with dynamic resource scaling to create a novel experimental paradigm. It involves deploying AI agent services with adaptive scaling mechanisms on a real-world, multi-technology (e.g., 5G, Wi-Fi, optical) converged network testbed. The core innovation is using the testbed's inherent unpredictability—real hardware bottlenecks, protocol interactions, and traffic volatility—as the primary driver for evaluating and iteratively improving the scaling algorithms. This results in resource management policies proven to be resilient under authentic, converged network conditions, bridging the gap between theoretical scaling models and deployable, reliable systems for fluctuating agent demands.

ideas.title: Incorporate malicious or faulty vehicle behavior
ideas.description: Investigate the framework's robustness against adversarial data or malfunctioning vehicles in the platoon.
ideas.sourceTitle: VoI-Driven Joint Optimization of Control and Communication in Vehicular Digital Twin Network

ideas.title: Apply federated learning for distributed resource optimization
ideas.description: Use federated learning to enable decentralized, privacy-preserving learning of resource scheduling policies across RSUs.

ideas.title: Resilient Co-Adaptation of Model and Resources under Non-Stationary and Adversarial Conditions
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework for agent deployment that unifies model and resource optimization within a dynamic, feedback-driven control loop. The system continuously monitors environmental conditions and agent performance, using adversarial simulation to identify failure modes and performance cliffs. It then employs online adaptation mechanisms to jointly reconfigure the agent's model parameters and its allocated system resources, ensuring robust and efficient operation despite unpredictability. The outcome is a deployment strategy that is not only optimized for a target setting but is resilient to real-world volatility and adversarial perturbations.

ideas.title: Federated Mixture of Experts with Strategic Experience Replay for Non-IID Agent Training
ideas.description: This system addresses the non-IID data challenge in decentralized agent training by embedding a mixture-of-experts model within a federated learning framework. Agents are clustered based on the characteristics of their local data streams, fostering the development of specialized experts, while a federated gating network learns to combine these experts adaptively. To prevent catastrophic forgetting of universal skills, a privacy-preserving generative model creates synthetic training data representing core competencies, which is periodically injected into all local training cycles via a federated experience replay mechanism. This approach enables collaborative improvement and specialization without compromising the robustness of the global agent policy.

ideas.title: Employ online learning for strategy adaptation

ideas.title: Consider adversarial channel conditions

ideas.title: Adaptive Deterministic Scheduling for Dynamic Flows with Graceful Guarantee Degradation
ideas.description: This research develops a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based scheduler that operates in environments with dynamic flow arrivals and departures. Its core innovation is an adaptive policy that, when the system cannot satisfy all requested deterministic guarantees (e.g., due to sudden congestion from new flows), does not simply reject flows but instead computes and offers a degraded yet still formally guaranteed service level. The scheduler learns to make these partial guarantee allocations in real-time, optimizing for overall utility and stability while maintaining a contractible, predictable performance floor for all admitted flows, thus enabling robust deterministic networking in truly dynamic scenarios.

ideas.title: Apply diffusion models for traffic generation

ideas.title: Adversarial Resilience Validation of E2eDRS via Continuous Stress-Testing on a Physical Converged Network
ideas.description: This research deploys the E2eDRS algorithm on a real-world, multi-technology converged network testbed integrated with a Network Perturbation Engine. The system subjects the algorithm to continuous, automated stress-testing by injecting a wide spectrum of realistic faults, adversarial traffic loads, and non-stationary network dynamics. This approach measures the algorithm's resilience, stability, and recovery capabilities under duress, moving beyond static validation. The outcome is a robustness certificate for E2eDRS, quantifying its performance boundaries and failure modes in a physically-grounded, adversarial environment.

ideas.title: Relax attacker's knowledge of defense strategy

ideas.title: Dynamic Task Partitioning for Collaborative Inference using Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This research expands the seed idea of cross-device collaborative inference optimization by drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically the theory of niche differentiation. In ecology, competing species coexist by specializing on different resources, forming a more efficient overall system. The analogous solution is the evolutionary process that leads to resource partitioning. Adapted to collaborative inference, we propose a framework where heterogeneous devices (smartphones, IoT sensors, edge servers) are treated as a computational ecosystem. The core idea is to develop dynamic task partitioning algorithms that go beyond simple layer-wise model splitting. Inspired by niche theory, these algorithms will identify and assign 'computational niches'—specific subtasks defined by operation type, data flow, and precision requirements—to the device whose architectural profile (e.g., GPU for parallel ops, NPU for quantized kernels, CPU for control flow) is most optimally suited for that niche at that moment, considering communication costs and device state constraints. This approach aims to maximize the collective efficiency of the device ensemble, optimizing the trade-off between computational speed and communication overhead.

ideas.title: Deterministic Low-Latency Communication for Critical Services in Adversarial Networks
ideas.description: This research investigates how to architect distributed agent services where the primary design goal is to achieve strict, deterministic upper bounds on end-to-end communication latency, even in the presence of adversarial or Byzantine network nodes. It challenges the conventional trade-off between security/resilience and performance by developing new protocols, consensus mechanisms, and network topologies that prioritize timely message delivery over computational throughput or perfect agreement. The core problem is to provide latency guarantees for critical control loops and state synchronization that hold under both benign conditions and targeted attacks aimed at inducing communication delays or disruptions.

ideas.title: Extend MoG to more complex wireless scenarios

ideas.title: Graph-Aware Neural Load Balancer for Converged Network Services
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel load balancing system for converged network services that integrates Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with cloud-native load balancing algorithms. The GNN continuously encodes the dynamic, multi-domain graph structure of the converged infrastructure—including compute nodes, network links, and service dependencies—into latent representations. These learned embeddings are then used as real-time input features to a neural load balancer, allowing it to make routing decisions that are not only based on traditional server metrics but also on the holistic topological context and relational patterns. This enables predictive load distribution that preemptively avoids bottlenecks in critical sub-graphs and optimizes for end-to-end service performance across the entire fabric.

ideas.title: Adaptive Risk-Aware Scheduling with Dynamic Security Posturing
ideas.description: This system integrates a dynamic security risk engine directly into the deterministic scheduler's decision loop. Instead of fixed constraints, it uses real-time threat intelligence and anomaly detection to compute a probabilistic risk score for each scheduling action. The optimization objective is reformulated to minimize a combined function of performance loss and quantified security risk, enabling proactive re-allocation under suspicion. This creates a resilient scheduling fabric that adapts to novel attacks without relying on a pre-defined, static threat model.

ideas.title: Study the dual: Attack on privacy, not integrity

ideas.title: Adapting Biological Neural Network Principles for Agent Communication
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that computational efficiency is the primary bottleneck for agent services. By drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, where biological neural networks evolved to prioritize communication latency (via myelination, synaptic optimization, and efficient signaling) over the raw processing power of individual neurons, we adapt this principle to multi-agent systems. The adapted research idea proposes designing agent service architectures where the communication substrate is the primary optimization target. This involves developing lightweight, fast-messaging protocols, strategically colocating agents to minimize physical network hops, and implementing predictive communication patterns to pre-emptively share data, thereby treating latency minimization as a first-class design constraint analogous to how brains optimize for signal propagation speed.

ideas.title: Apply multi-agent deep reinforcement learning

ideas.title: Meta-Trained Robust Fast-Adaptation (Meta-RFA) for Generalizable DRL
ideas.description: This research develops a meta-reinforcement learning system that explicitly optimizes for both rapid convergence and robust out-of-distribution generalization. The core architecture trains an agent across a dynamically generated curriculum of procedurally varied environments, while an adversarial module systematically exposes weaknesses to create a broad distribution of training challenges. The learning objective is modified to prioritize the acquisition of policies that can adapt quickly to new tasks while remaining stable and effective when faced with unseen perturbations, noise, or structural changes in the environment. The final output is not just a faster-learning agent, but a robust fast-adaptation policy that generalizes reliably beyond its training distribution.

ideas.title: Federated Multi-Agent Learning with Decentralized Orchestration
ideas.description: This research synthesizes federated learning for agent training with decentralized orchestration to create a system where a population of autonomous agents collaboratively improves through local learning and periodic model aggregation, but without any central server for coordination or scheduling. The core innovation is a distributed consensus protocol, inspired by federated averaging but extended to the control plane, where agents themselves form transient committees to propose, validate, and schedule training rounds, model aggregation events, and membership changes. This eliminates the single point of failure and control inherent in classic federated learning, making the system resilient to node churn and adaptable to heterogeneous network topologies. The result is a scalable framework for privacy-preserving, collective agent learning in fully decentralized environments like ad-hoc device networks or cross-organizational collaborations without trusted third parties.

ideas.title: Accuracy-Constrained Pareto Optimization for Multi-Agent Systems
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel paradigm where a specified minimum model accuracy is treated as a hard constraint for an agent or a collective of agents. Within this constraint, a unified optimizer dynamically explores the joint space of model-level parameters (e.g., pruning, quantization) and system-level resources (e.g., compute allocation, batching, routing) to construct a Pareto frontier of optimal operating points. This allows the system to maximally vary non-accuracy service qualities—such as response time, throughput, and cost—to achieve extreme resource efficiency or performance on secondary metrics, depending on real-time context. The core synergy is a co-design optimization loop that makes globally efficient trade-offs across the model-system stack, which isolated optimizations cannot achieve.

ideas.description: This research synthesizes a multi-agent system, where heterogeneous devices (CPUs, GPUs, NPUs, etc.) are cooperative agents, with a deterministic scheduling model that explicitly incorporates security constraints. The core innovation is treating security mechanisms (like attestation, encryption, or isolation) as first-class, schedulable resources managed by the device agents. This allows the system to co-optimize for functional performance, timing guarantees, and dynamic security postures, generating schedules that are both temporally deterministic and resilient to specified threat models, such as side-channel attacks or compromised device agents.

ideas.title: Dynamic QoS Envelopes with Transparent Service Guarantees
ideas.description: This approach redefines the optimization problem by treating user-facing service quality not as a free variable but as a bounded, multi-dimensional space—a QoS envelope. The system can dynamically adjust operating points within this envelope (e.g., trading latency for confidence, or completeness for throughput) to maximize resource efficiency. Crucially, it must communicate its current operating point and guarantees to the user or downstream system in real time, enabling informed adaptation. This maintains the original goal of efficiency gains while ensuring predictability, trust, and safety by making the service-quality trade-offs explicit and bounded.

ideas.title: Inverse-Scale Agents: Single-Device Systems that Dynamically Maximize Local Resource Utilization
ideas.description: This research direction explores the design of a complete, self-contained agent service that operates exclusively on a single, resource-constrained device (e.g., a smartphone, edge sensor, or personal computer). Instead of minimizing its footprint, the agent is architected to continuously and deliberately maximize the utilization of the device's available computational cycles, memory bandwidth, storage I/O, and intermittent network connectivity. The core challenge is to develop adaptive algorithms that can dynamically reallocate these saturated local resources among perception, planning, and action modules in real-time, enabling the agent to exhibit unexpectedly sophisticated, context-aware behavior by treating the device's full resource envelope as a malleable substrate to be fully exploited rather than a fixed limit to be respected.

ideas.title: Apply Swarm Intelligence-inspired Multi-Agent DRL with Stigmergic Communication for Distributed Scheduling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the swarm intelligence concept of stigmergy—where agents coordinate indirectly by modifying and sensing a shared environment—to multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for distributed control. The analogous solution from biology and swarm robotics uses environmental cues (like pheromone trails) to enable decentralized optimization. Adapted to the research context, we propose a multi-agent DRL framework where agents for end-to-end scheduling do not communicate directly. Instead, they perceive and update a shared, structured 'digital pheromone' state (e.g., a feature vector representing global workload or resource utilization). Each agent's DRL policy is trained to make local scheduling decisions based on this signal, fostering emergent, coordinated behavior that optimizes global scheduling objectives without centralized control or complex peer-to-peer communication protocols.

ideas.title: Circuit-Switched Collaborative Inference for Deterministic Multi-Agent QoS
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework for cross-device collaborative inference that integrates the concept of circuit-switched network reservations directly into the computational orchestration layer. The system treats critical data flows and computational stages within an inference pipeline as circuits requiring guaranteed resources, dynamically establishing these virtual circuits across heterogeneous devices (CPUs, GPUs, NPUs) alongside optimized task partitioning and scheduling. This ensures deterministic latency and bandwidth for inter-agent communication and key computational blocks, making the system robust to network contention and device load variability. The result is a hybrid approach that co-optimizes for both computational efficiency and hard, predictable performance guarantees, enabling new classes of time-sensitive, distributed AI applications at the edge.

ideas.title: Optimize for heterogeneous data priorities and sizes

ideas.title: Incentive-Aligned and Verifiable Collaborative Inference with Strategic Device Modeling
ideas.description: This system optimizes cross-device collaborative inference by formally addressing the strategic interactions between self-interested device owners. It employs lightweight cryptographic protocols, such as verifiable inference snippets or proof-of-learning, to allow devices to prove their computational contribution without revealing raw data. A decentralized credit mechanism, potentially blockchain-based, tracks contributions and distributes rewards or inference priority, ensuring fairness and disincentivizing free-riding. The optimization now jointly solves for technical efficiency (latency, accuracy) and economic equilibrium, creating a robust system sustainable in real-world, non-cooperative environments.

ideas.title: Cross-device collaborative inference optimization
ideas.description: Optimize collaborative inference across heterogeneous devices considering computational and communication constraints.

ideas.title: Predictive Resource Governance for Communication-Critical Agent Services
ideas.description: This system ensures robust low-latency communication by dynamically guarding against computational bottlenecks. It continuously profiles task demands and predicts resource needs, maintaining a fast path for communication-light interactions. Computationally intensive operations are intelligently offloaded to elastic backend services without blocking the primary communication channel. This approach guarantees latency service-level agreements (SLAs) are met even under heterogeneous or adversarial workloads.

ideas.title: Query-Optimized Agent Planning for Real-Time Network Control
ideas.description: This research fuses database query optimization with autonomous agent planning to create an intelligent controller for a physical, multi-technology converged network. The agent treats network state queries and control action sequences as a complex 'query plan' to be optimized, using techniques like cost-based optimization and join ordering. Crucially, this optimization engine is continuously validated and refined on the real-world testbed, where the actual latency, throughput, and failure modes of the physical hardware provide the ground-truth 'cost model' for planning. This creates a synergistic loop: the testbed validates the agent's plans in reality, while the agent's optimized planning enables more sophisticated, efficient, and adaptive control of the testbed itself.

ideas.title: Scheduling with Bayesian Belief Networks for Noisy Resource Abstraction
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption of perfect resource abstraction in heterogeneous network scheduling by drawing an analogy from robotics sensor fusion. In robotics, systems integrate data from imperfect, heterogeneous sensors using probabilistic models like Bayesian Belief Networks to maintain a robust state estimate. We adapt this solution by proposing a scheduler that treats resource abstractions (e.g., latency from satellite, bandwidth from 5G) as noisy inputs characterized by probability distributions. The scheduler uses a BBN to fuse these inputs, explicitly modeling uncertainty and the reliability of each abstraction source. This allows for scheduling decisions that maximize the probability of meeting application requirements, transforming the problem from one of deterministic optimization under perfect models to one of robust decision-making under quantified uncertainty.

ideas.title: Inverse: Derive network requirements from applications

ideas.title: Treating Model Accuracy as a Fixed Constraint via Metabolic Rate Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that model service quality must be uniformly high or stable. Drawing an analogy from comparative physiology, where organisms maintain a roughly fixed basal metabolic rate and dynamically allocate energy to various life functions, we propose treating a model's target accuracy (or a core performance metric) as a similarly fixed constraint. The analogous solution from biology shows that fitness is maximized not by maximizing every function but by optimal allocation within a fixed budget. Adapted to ML research, this means designing agents or model-serving systems where, once a strict accuracy/loss constraint is set, all other quality-of-service dimensions—latency, computational cost, communication bandwidth, detail of explanations—become dynamically tunable variables. The system's goal shifts from 'achieve accuracy X with minimal resources' to 'given you must achieve accuracy X, now vary everything else aggressively to optimize for total resource efficiency (energy, compute time, cost) across varying environments and tasks.' This could lead to novel adaptive controllers, training regimes that embed this trade-off flexibility, and efficiency gains similar to those seen in biological systems.

ideas.title: Integrate Security as a First-Class Constraint in Deterministic Scheduling via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling (ENM) to integrate security into deterministic scheduling. In ENM, a species' viable habitat is determined by mapping environmental constraints and threat factors onto a geographic resource landscape. Analogously, we model computational tasks as having a 'security niche' defined by quantified security constraints (e.g., isolation, trust boundaries) and threat model parameters (e.g., risk of co-residency attacks). These are incorporated as first-class dimensions into the resource abstraction and scheduling optimization. The scheduler's objective is to deterministically place tasks on the resource landscape in a way that satisfies both temporal deadlines and the security niche requirements, thereby finding schedules that are optimal for both performance and security posture.

ideas.title: Maximum Entropy Production Agents: Resource Optimization via Thermodynamic Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the Maximum Entropy Production Principle (MEPP) from non-equilibrium thermodynamics and ecology to the problem of designing agents that maximize resource use. In analogous natural systems, structures evolve to dissipate available energy gradients as rapidly as possible (e.g., ecosystems maximizing nutrient cycling). The adapted solution is to design AI agents whose core objective is not task efficiency, but the maximization of productive entropy production within their computational environment. This involves developing novel agent architectures and reward functions that incentivize the discovery and maintenance of states which utilize all available CPU, memory, network bandwidth, and storage resources at their maximum sustainable throughput. The goal is to create agents that actively seek out and exploit resource gradients, transforming idle capacity into useful, structured computational work and network activity, thereby providing a scaling-oriented counterpart to traditional efficiency-focused agents.
